proposals.title: A First-Principles Framework for Biosignature Preservation: Mapping Thermodynamic Habitability Envelopes onto the Igneous Stratigraphy of Jezero Crater, Mars
proposals.motivation: Current Mars exploration, particularly by Perseverance in Jezero crater, seeks biosignatures but relies heavily on terrestrial, neutral-pH analog models. This is limiting for Mars, where aqueous alteration of igneous rocks, as evidenced by the Máaz and Séítah formations, likely created fluids with extreme pH and salinity. A chemistry-agnostic model is needed to predict where evidence of life adapted to such transient or persistent extremes could be preserved in the geological record.
proposals.hypothesis: Thermodynamic and kinetic constraints define stable, non-equilibrium envelopes capable of supporting an abstract metabolism; these envelopes can be spatially mapped onto specific igneous terrains, like those in Jezero, to predict where biosignatures from extreme-condition life are most likely to be sequestered and preserved.
proposals.method: We will use Gibbs free energy minimization models (e.g., CHNOSZ, Geochemist's Workbench) to calculate chemical affinity for abstract redox and phosphorylation reactions across continuous gradients of pH (0-14), salinity (0-6 molal), and temperature (0-120°C). Kinetic models for organic degradation (e.g., using transition state theory) will define preservation potential. These theoretical 'envelopes' will be integrated with mineralogical and stratigraphic data from Jezero (from Perseverance, orbital data) to create predictive maps of high-probability biosignature preservation sites within the crater's floor units.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a quantitative, first-principles tool for targeting biosignature search on Mars, moving beyond pattern-matching to Earth. It will generate specific, testable predictions for the Máaz and Séítah formations, directly informing the ongoing analysis of Perseverance samples and future mission planning. It establishes a generalizable framework applicable to other igneous-hosted aqueous systems, like the subsurface of icy moons.
proposals.novelty: This approach decouples habitability prediction from specific Earth-like biochemistry, instead using fundamental thermodynamic drivers. It directly links abstract metabolic theory to concrete, in-situ geological mapping of a currently active exploration site on Mars.

proposals.title: Constraining Post-Noachian Aqueous Episodes at Jezero Crater: A Reactive Transport Model for Diagenetic Fingerprints in Igneous Substrates
proposals.motivation: Recent findings from the Mars 2020 Perseverance rover confirm the igneous nature of the Jezero crater floor (Máaz, Séítah formations) and reveal evidence for later aqueous alteration. This challenges the canonical view of a permanently dry post-Noachian Mars, suggesting instead transient habitable 'oases.' However, current assessments of these wet episodes remain qualitative, lacking quantitative constraints on their duration and geochemical intensity needed to test new climatic paradigms.
proposals.hypothesis: Short-lived (10^3-10^5 year) episodes of groundwater upwelling and lake formation into Jezero's igneous crater floor produce distinct, diagnosable diagenetic mineral assemblages and isotopic profiles that are a function of event duration and fluid chemistry.
proposals.method: We will employ the CrunchFlow reactive transport code to simulate fluid-rock interaction in a 1D column representing a porous basaltic substrate (modeled after Máaz/Séítah compositions). Simulations will inject evolved martian groundwater compositions under varied durations, flow rates, and temperatures. The model will track the formation of secondary phases (e.g., carbonates, sulfates, phyllosilicates) and simulate associated isotopic (e.g., O, C) depth profiles that could be preserved in the rock record. Model outputs will be directly compared to mineralogical data from Perseverance (SuperCam, PIXL) and orbital hyperspectral imagery.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce the first quantitative framework linking observable mineralogic and geochemical signatures in martian igneous terrains to specific hydrogeologic event durations. It will provide testable predictions for Perseverance's ongoing sample analysis and directly inform the selection of samples with the highest potential to record brief habitable conditions. The results will critically evaluate the 'inverted climatic view' of post-Noachian Mars.
proposals.novelty: While reactive transport modeling is established in terrestrial geochemistry, its application to quantitatively define the geochemical legacy of *transient* martian wet episodes—specifically within the context of the newly characterized igneous crust at Jezero—is novel. This moves beyond identifying alteration to explicitly modeling its timescale.

proposals.title: A Comparative Geochemical and Mineralogical Investigation of Organic Carbon Preservation in Lacustrine Mudstones of Gale and Jezero Craters, Mars
proposals.motivation: The Curiosity rover has identified fine-grained, Fe-rich mudstones in Gale crater's Yellowknife Bay formation, which represent ancient lacustrine sediments with demonstrated potential to preserve organic matter. However, a systematic, comparative analysis of organic carbon occurrence and preservation mechanisms between Gale and the similarly ancient lacustrine environment of Jezero crater is lacking. Such a comparison is critical for understanding the regional versus global distribution and taphonomy of Martian organic carbon in habitable paleoenvironments.
proposals.hypothesis: Fine-grained, clay- and iron oxide-bearing lacustrine sediments in Gale crater contain a higher concentration and diversity of preserved indigenous organic carbon compounds compared to the more compositionally stratified, igneous-influenced sedimentary units of Jezero crater, due to differences in primary depositional conditions and diagenetic mineralogy.
proposals.method: We will integrate pre-2026 geochemical and mineralogical datasets from MSL Curiosity (SAM, CheMin) for Gale crater with analogous datasets from the Mars 2020 Perseverance rover (SHERLOC, PIXL, SuperCam) for Jezero crater's Máaz and Séítah formations. The analysis will focus on correlating specific organic carbon detections (e.g., chlorobenzene, thiophenes from SAM) with host rock mineralogy (e.g., clay, sulfate, iron oxide abundances from CheMin). We will employ statistical clustering algorithms (e.g., principal component analysis) to identify geochemical and mineralogical discriminants between organic-bearing and organic-poor mudstone facies, testing for links to igneous provenance and diagenetic overprinting as seen in Jezero's stratigraphy.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce the first direct, comparative assessment of organic carbon preservation in the two primary lacustrine records explored on Mars. It will constrain the environmental and mineralogical controls on organic preservation, distinguishing between global phenomena and local geological context. The findings will critically inform the selection of samples for eventual return from Jezero by identifying how its unique igneous-sedimentary stratigraphy compares to Gale's more classic sedimentary record for biosignature potential.
proposals.novelty: While organic carbon has been sought in both craters, no study has yet performed a rigorous cross-mission synthesis to test how fundamental differences in basin geology—Gale's long-lived sedimentary lake versus Jezero's igneous floor and stratified sediments—control organic inventory. This leverages McLennan's expertise in Jezero's igneous terrain to re-contextualize Gale's organics.

proposals.title: Convergent Biosignature Detection in Igneous-Derived Sediments: A Multi-Scale Pattern Recognition Framework for Identifying Potential Microbial Mat Fossils in Jezero Crater, Mars
proposals.motivation: The Perseverance rover has identified compositionally stratified igneous lithologies (Máaz, Séítah) at Jezero crater, which may have interacted with water to form alteration minerals and potential habitable microenvironments. Current biosignature detection methods often rely on single-scale morphological or geochemical data, leading to ambiguous interpretations. A robust, multi-sensor framework is needed to reduce false positives and systematically search for evidence of ancient microbial mats within these altered igneous terrains.
proposals.hypothesis: Potential microbial mat fossils in Jezero's igneous-derived sediments can be reliably discriminated from abiotic sedimentary structures through the convergent detection of diagnostic morphological textures at the decimeter scale and spatially correlated geochemical patterns at the micron-to-pixel scale.
proposals.method: We will develop a two-stage adversarial deep learning framework. First, a convolutional neural network (CNN) will analyze orbital (HiRISE) and rover (Mastcam-Z, WATSON) imagery to identify candidate macroscopic textures (e.g., wavy laminations, tufts). Second, for these candidates, a pixel-registered analysis of SuperCam LIBS/Raman and PIXL X-ray fluorescence maps will be performed using a spectral graph convolutional network (SGCN) to detect diagnostic spatial correlations in elements (e.g., C, S, P) and minerals (e.g., phyllosilicates, carbonates). The system will be trained and validated on an extensive pre-2026 terrestrial analog library containing both biotic (e.g., Archean cherts, modern mats) and abiotic sedimentary structures.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce a novel, rigorously validated detection framework for the Mars 2020 mission, directly applicable to data from the Máaz and Séítah formations. It will establish a more stringent, multi-scale protocol for biosignature assessment in igneous-hosted paleoenvironments. The resulting open-source model and candidate map will prioritize targets for potential sample return.
proposals.novelty: This approach moves beyond morphology-only models by enforcing a mandatory convergence of macro-scale texture and micro-scale geochemical pattern recognition, an integration not yet systematically applied to Martian igneous terrains. The adversarial training on a comprehensive analog library specifically forces the model to learn discriminative features that separate biotic from abiotic processes in sedimentary structures.

proposals.title: A Deep Learning Framework for Isolating Potential Biosignatures from Igneous Substrates: A Seismic Denoising Analogy Applied to Perseverance Rover Data at Jezero Crater
proposals.motivation: The search for biosignatures on Mars, particularly within the ancient, water-altered igneous rocks of Jezero crater, is fundamentally a signal-in-noise problem. The Perseverance rover's rich spectral and imagery datasets are dominated by strong abiotic mineralogical and textural patterns, which can obscure subtle, spatially correlated signatures potentially indicative of past life. Current manual and spectral unmixing methods struggle with the complexity and scale of this data, risking missed detections or false positives.
proposals.hypothesis: Convolutional Neural Network (CNN) architectures, adapted from seismic signal denoising, can be trained to isolate weak, spatially coherent spectral-textural patterns in Martian rover data that are distinct from pervasive igneous and alteration mineralogy, thereby highlighting high-priority targets for biosignature analysis.
proposals.method: We will adapt a U-Net CNN architecture, proven for seismic image segmentation and denoising, to process SuperCam VISIR and Mastcam-Z multispectral image cubes. The model will be trained on a synthetic dataset pairing high-fidelity spectral models of microbialite and microbially induced sedimentary structure (MISS) textures with simulated Martian environmental noise (dust coatings, illumination variance). Transfer learning will then be applied to Perseverance data from the well-characterized Máaz and Séítah formations, using manually identified abiotic mineralogical features (e.g., olivine, pyroxene) as the 'noise' class. Model performance will be validated against known mineralogical boundaries and PIXL/ SHERLOC co-analyses.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce a novel, automated tool for prioritizing biosignature detection targets from rover-scale datasets, directly supporting Perseverance's sample caching mission. It will establish a quantitative framework for distinguishing biotic from abiotic spatial-spectral patterns in igneous terrains, advancing the analytical toolkit for planetary in situ science.
proposals.novelty: While CNNs are used in planetary geology for landform classification, this proposal uniquely adapts seismic denoising architectures—designed for weak, structured signals in noisy time-series data—to the specific challenge of isolating potential biosignature textures from complex igneous backgrounds, a direct methodological analogy not yet explored.

proposals.title: A Machine Learning Framework for Detecting Potential Microbial Mat Biosignatures in the Fluvial-Lacustrine Sediments of Jezero Crater, Mars
proposals.motivation: The Mars 2020 Perseverance rover is exploring Jezero crater, a paleolake basin with well-preserved sedimentary deposits, making it a prime target in the search for ancient Martian life. Microbial mats on early Earth formed distinctive macroscopic and textural biosignatures known as microbially induced sedimentary structures (MISS). Developing robust, data-driven methods to identify potential MISS analogs in Perseverance's high-resolution imagery and spectral data is critical for prioritizing samples for eventual return to Earth.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that supervised machine learning models, trained on terrestrial analog datasets of confirmed MISS, can identify textural and spectral patterns in Perseverance rover data that are statistically distinct from abiogenic sedimentary features, highlighting high-priority targets for in-situ analysis.
proposals.method: We will compile a curated image and spectral library of MISS from terrestrial analog sites (e.g., siliciclastic tidal flats, alkaline lakes). Using Perseverance's Mastcam-Z, SuperCam RMI, and WATSON images of the Jezero crater floor and delta, we will extract multi-scale textural features (e.g., using Local Binary Patterns, GLCM) and, where available, associated spectral features. A convolutional neural network (CNN) architecture, specifically a ResNet-50 model pre-trained on ImageNet and fine-tuned on our analog dataset, will be trained to classify image patches. Model performance will be validated against known abiogenic sedimentary textures identified in the Máaz and Séítah formations.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce the first quantitative, model-driven framework for MISS detection applied to Mars rover data, directly supporting the Perseverance mission's astrobiological goals. It will generate a map of high-probability biosignature candidate locations within Jezero, informing strategic sampling decisions for the Mars Sample Return campaign. The open-source model and dataset will establish a benchmark for automated biosignature detection in planetary science.
proposals.novelty: While previous work has described Martian sedimentology, applying a trained CNN model specifically for MISS detection to Perseverance's diverse igneous and sedimentary terrain is novel. The approach integrates pre-2026 rover data with terrestrial analog training in a way that moves beyond qualitative comparison to provide probabilistic, feature-based identification.

proposals.title: Inverting Global Early Mars Climate States from Multi-Site Geological Constraints: A GCM Ensemble Approach
proposals.motivation: Reconciling the geological evidence for a potentially warm and wet early Mars with climate models that struggle to produce such conditions remains a central paradox. Current models are often constrained by data from single sites (e.g., Jezero crater), leading to non-unique solutions. A systematic inversion that integrates diverse geological constraints from multiple landing sites (e.g., Gale, Jezero, Meridiani) and orbital data is needed to identify the global boundary conditions capable of producing a consistent climate history.
proposals.hypothesis: An ensemble of global surface boundary condition maps (topography, albedo, ice distribution) exists that, when used as input to a Mars Global Climate Model (GCM), can produce climate states simultaneously consistent with the diverse fluvial, lacustrine, and alteration mineralogy records at multiple, geographically dispersed Martian sites.
proposals.method: We will generate an ensemble of probabilistic early Mars surface maps using a Markov Chain Monte Carlo (MCMC) algorithm, varying topography (from paleo-DEMs), albedo, thermal inertia, and surface water/ice distribution within pre-2026 uncertainty bounds. Each ensemble member will serve as a boundary condition for the LMD Mars GCM to simulate surface temperatures, atmospheric circulation, and precipitation/snowfall patterns. The GCM outputs will be compared against a compiled geological likelihood function derived from site-specific data (e.g., fluvial feature ages, mineralogy from CRISM and rover instruments, crater floor compositions). The ensemble will be iteratively weighted and resampled to identify the family of boundary conditions that maximize the joint likelihood across all sites.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce the first probabilistically constrained ensemble of global early Mars climate states directly inverted from multi-site geological data. It will move beyond site-specific 'storyline' models to identify which climatic scenarios are globally viable, directly testing the 'warm and wet' vs. 'cold and icy' paradigms. The resulting boundary condition maps will provide testable predictions for future missions regarding the distribution of aqueous alteration and sedimentary deposits.
proposals.novelty: The approach is novel in its formal, quantitative inversion of a global climate model against a distributed geological likelihood function, rather than forward-modeling from a single local datum. It treats the global climate state as an unknown to be solved for, using the planet's geological record as a multi-point constraint.

proposals.title: Constraining the Igneous Stratigraphy and Weathering Regime of the Jezero Catchment Through Inverse Modeling of Perseverance Sediment Core Compositions
proposals.motivation: The Perseverance rover has confirmed the igneous nature of the crater floor units (Máaz, Séítah) and is collecting sedimentary cores from the Jezero delta. These sediments are a time-integrated sample of the eroded catchment geology, which remains unobserved directly. Decoding this sedimentary record is critical to reconstructing the broader igneous crustal stratigraphy and paleoenvironmental conditions, including water-rock interaction processes, in the region surrounding Jezero crater.
proposals.hypothesis: The bulk mineralogy and geochemistry of the Jezero delta sediments can be inverted to reveal the relative proportions and alteration histories of distinct igneous source units within the catchment, specifically the compositionally stratified Máaz and Séítah formations and any other unsampled lithologies.
proposals.method: We will develop a quantitative mixing model, treating the sediment composition as a linear combination of potential source end-members. End-member compositions will be defined using Perseverance SuperCam and PIXL data from the crater floor igneous units (Máaz, Séítah). The inverse problem will be solved using a Markov Chain Monte Carlo (MCMC) algorithm to find the probabilistic distribution of source contributions that best fits the sediment core data, incorporating uncertainties from both source and sediment measurements. We will test models with and without a weathering correction factor to assess the role of aqueous alteration.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first quantitative estimate of the hidden catchment geology feeding Jezero lake, linking known in-situ igneous units to the sedimentary record. It will constrain the regional igneous diversity and the intensity of pre-depositional weathering, offering a novel test for orbital-based geologic maps and models of martian crustal evolution.
proposals.novelty: While source-to-sink models are common on Earth, applying a formal probabilistic inversion to martian sedimentary systems using actual rover-derived end-members is novel. This moves beyond qualitative comparison to a quantitative, testable framework for extrapolating local rover observations to regional geology.

proposals.title: Cryo-Vitrification and Cryo-TEM Analysis of Smectite-Organic Interactions Under Simulated Jezero Crater Conditions
proposals.motivation: The Perseverance rover has identified igneous-derived, compositionally stratified units in Jezero crater containing clay minerals, which are prime targets for biosignature preservation. Pre-2026 analyses of Martian meteorites and analog studies suggest organic molecules can adsorb to clay surfaces, but the atomic-scale nature of these interactions under realistic Martian diagenetic conditions (low temperature, low water activity, brine chemistry) remains speculative. Understanding these preserved nanoscale interfaces is critical for interpreting potential organic records in returned samples.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that rapid cryo-vitrification of smectite-organic microreactors under simulated Jezero subsurface conditions will preserve the native conformation of adsorbed organics and mineral surface terminations, revealing distinct, condition-dependent binding motifs not observable with conventional ex-situ analysis.
proposals.method: We will design a microfluidic environmental cell that simulates Jezero-relevant brine chemistry (e.g., perchlorate, sulfate) and temperature regimes. Interactions between synthesized Fe/Mg-smectites (analogous to Séítah/Máaz compositions) and model organic compounds (e.g., carboxylic acids, polycyclic aromatics) will be initiated within the cell and rapidly vitrified via plunge-freezing into liquid ethane. Cryo-preserved samples will be transferred to a cryo-transmission electron microscope (cryo-TEM) for high-resolution imaging and selected-area electron diffraction. Correlative cryo-electron energy loss spectroscopy (cryo-EELS) and scanning transmission X-ray microscopy (STXM) at synchrotron facilities will map organic functional group chemistry and redox state at the clay-organic interface.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a novel experimental protocol for capturing transient mineral-organic interaction states relevant to Martian subsurface environments. It will provide the first direct, nanoscale visualizations and spectroscopic maps of how specific Martian brine chemistries influence organic adsorption and preservation on igneous-derived clay surfaces, directly informing the interpretation of Perseverance's in-situ analyses and future returned sample science.
proposals.novelty: While cryo-EM is standard in structural biology, its application to preserve and interrogate *in-situ* geochemical interfaces under simulated planetary conditions is novel. This approach moves beyond post-reaction analysis to capture the native state of mineral-organic interactions, analogous to solving the structure of a hydrated macromolecular complex.

proposals.title: Atomic-Scale Adsorption and Stability of Organic Molecules on Smectite Clays Under Simulated Jezero Crater Conditions
proposals.motivation: The Perseverance rover has identified smectite clay minerals within the igneous stratigraphy of Jezero crater, a primary target in the search for ancient Martian biosignatures. Pre-2026 studies show clays can adsorb and preserve organic matter, but the specific atomic-scale interactions under relevant Martian conditions (e.g., low water activity, UV radiation, oxidizing regolith) are poorly constrained. Understanding these interactions is critical for interpreting potential organic detections by Perseverance and assessing the preservation potential of biosignatures in Martian clay-bearing terrains.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the specific crystallographic faces and interlayer chemistry of Jezero-relevant smectites (e.g., Fe-rich saponite) will selectively adsorb and stabilize certain organic functional groups (e.g., carboxylic acids) under simulated Martian diagenetic conditions, while other configurations will lead to rapid oxidative degradation.
proposals.method: We will employ density functional theory (DFT) calculations using the Vienna Ab initio Simulation Package (VASP) to model adsorption energies and electronic structure changes for a suite of organic molecules (e.g., acetate, glycine, polycyclic aromatics) on smectite basal and edge surfaces. Simulations will incorporate Martian-relevant conditions by adjusting oxidative potentials and water monolayer coverage. These atomic-scale predictions will guide laboratory experiments using synthetic Fe-saponite exposed to organics in a Mars Simulation Chamber (low pressure, CO2 atmosphere, UV irradiation), with subsequent analysis by time-resolved in situ Raman spectroscopy and synchrotron-based X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy to validate degradation pathways.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a mechanistic, atomic-scale framework for predicting which organic-clay complexes are most likely to survive in Martian near-surface environments. It will directly inform the interpretation of data from the SHERLOC and PIXL instruments on Perseverance by linking specific mineralogical contexts to organic preservation potential. The results will also guide the selection of the most promising samples for eventual return to Earth.
proposals.novelty: While past studies have examined organics on generic clays, this proposal uniquely integrates DFT modeling of Fe-rich smectites—specifically implicated in Jezero's igneous stratigraphy—with experimental validation under simultaneous, dynamic Martian environmental stresses, moving beyond single-factor studies.

proposals.title: Deciphering the Igneous Crustal Record in Jezero Sediments: A Process-Based Model for Weathering Under Extreme Climates and Implications for Paleo-Habitability
proposals.motivation: The Perseverance rover has identified compositionally stratified igneous units (Máaz, Séítah) in Jezero crater, providing a known source for the crater's sedimentary delta. However, pre-2026 models for early Mars propose transient acidic or saline aqueous conditions that could severely alter weathering products. This creates a critical gap: we cannot accurately reconstruct paleo-habitability from sedimentary geochemistry without first quantifying how these extreme climates filter and bias the igneous source signal.
proposals.hypothesis: The sedimentary record of Jezero crater is not a passive average of its igneous basement but is geochemically transformed by extreme, climate-driven weathering regimes, systematically biasing paleo-environmental and biosignature preservation proxies.
proposals.method: We will integrate the high-resolution geochemical and mineralogical datasets from Perseverance (SuperCam, PIXL) on the Máaz and Séítah igneous formations with terrestrial experimental and analog data on acidic/saline weathering. A quantitative forward model will be developed using a reactive transport architecture (e.g., PHREEQC, CrunchFlow) to simulate sediment generation under proposed Martian climate scenarios. The model outputs will be statistically compared (using principal component and mass balance algorithms) to the actual compositional stratigraphy of the Jezero sedimentary delta to identify diagnostic weathering overprints.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce a process-based framework to deconvolve climate weathering signals from igneous provenance in Martian sediments. It will directly inform the interpretation of samples cached by Perseverance for Earth return and provide a new criterion for assessing the fidelity of sedimentary biosignature preservation on early Mars.
proposals.novelty: This moves beyond simple provenance studies by dynamically linking the known igneous crust of Jezero to its sedimentary record through explicit climate-forced weathering models, testing the habitability archive itself for systematic bias.

proposals.title: Constraining the Representativeness of Exposed Granulite Terranes for the Martian Middle Crust: A Phosphorus Bioavailability and Sediment Provenance Study
proposals.motivation: The Perseverance rover's discovery of compositionally stratified igneous rocks in Jezero crater, Mars, suggests a complex magmatic history and a potentially evolved crust. On Earth, granulite terrains are often used as analogs for the deep crust, but their representativeness for the planetary middle crust—a key reservoir for bio-essential elements like phosphorus—is debated. Directly testing this representativity is crucial for accurately modeling deep crustal contributions to martian surface geochemistry and past nutrient availability, which directly informs assessments of past habitability.
proposals.hypothesis: The phosphorus concentration, mineral speciation (e.g., apatite vs. monazite), and resultant bioavailability signature in modern sediments derived from exposed granulite terrains will match the geochemical signature predicted for a theoretical middle crustal protolith, supporting the use of these terrains as representative analogs.
proposals.method: We will collect modern fluvial sediments from major rivers draining well-characterized granulite terrains (e.g., the Lewisian Complex, Scotland; the Napier Complex, Antarctica). Samples will be analyzed for bulk phosphorus concentration via ICP-MS. Mineralogical speciation of P-bearing phases will be determined using automated SEM-EDS (Mineral Liberation Analysis) and micro-XRF mapping. Bioavailable phosphorus will be quantified using a sequential extraction protocol targeting labile, Fe-oxide bound, and apatite-bound fractions. The resulting geochemical signature will be statistically compared to predicted middle and lower crustal compositions from global crustal models and experimental petrology.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first quantitative, process-based test of granulite representativity using a key biogeochemical element. It will directly link deep crustal petrology to surface sediment geochemistry, refining terrestrial analogs for Mars. The data will create a new framework for interpreting remote or in-situ sediment compositions on Mars in terms of their deep crustal provenance and potential nutrient contributions.
proposals.novelty: While granulites are widely studied, their representativeness is typically assumed, not tested via the geochemical fingerprint of their erosional products. This approach uniquely uses phosphorus bioavailability—a functional property relevant to habitability—as a specific tracer to bridge deep crustal composition and surface processes.

proposals.title: Quantifying the Supercontinent-Phosphorus Nexus: A High-Resolution Geochemical Record of Nutrient Flux from Assembly to Breakup
proposals.motivation: The supercontinent cycle is hypothesized to be a primary pacemaker of Earth's long-term climate and biogeochemical evolution, but direct, quantitative geochemical evidence linking tectonic modes to nutrient cycling is lacking. Phosphorus (P) is the ultimate limiting nutrient for the biosphere over geologic timescales, and its continental weathering flux is critically sensitive to tectonic setting and paleogeography. This research directly tests this foundational idea by moving beyond conceptual models to provide the first calibrated record of P sequestration and liberation tied to specific supercontinent cycles.
proposals.hypothesis: Supercontinent assembly sequesters bioavailable phosphorus in continental interiors through reduced weathering of extensive cratonic landmasses and enhanced sediment trapping, while continental breakup and dispersal liberate P through increased continental runoff, enhanced silicate weathering, and the exposure of fresh igneous provinces, generating first-order pulses in global biogeochemical cycles.
proposals.method: We will integrate pre-2026 geodynamic models of supercontinent paleogeography (e.g., from the PALEOMAP project) with a targeted high-resolution geochemical campaign. Using X-ray fluorescence (XRF) and sequential extraction protocols on carefully selected fluvial, lacustrine, and marginal marine sedimentary sequences that span the assembly and breakup phases of Rodinia and Pangea, we will quantify total P, reactive P phases, and P mineralogy. These data will be statistically analyzed against modeled tectonic and paleoclimatic parameters (e.g., continental runoff area, topographic relief) to develop a quantitative scaling relationship between tectonic mode and P flux.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce the first empirical, stratigraphically-resolved record directly linking the supercontinent cycle to the planetary phosphorus cycle. It will transform the supercontinent-biosphere hypothesis from a conceptual framework into a calibrated, predictive model. The findings will provide a tectonic null hypothesis for interpreting Precambrian and Phanerozoic biogeochemical records and inform models of long-term planetary habitability, including for Mars.
proposals.novelty: While the link between tectonics and weathering is established, the direct, high-resolution geochemical quantification of phosphorus speciation and flux across specific supercontinent transitions is novel. The approach synergizes detailed sediment geochemistry—a core strength of the researcher's work on Martian terrains—with global geodynamic models in a way not previously attempted.

proposals.title: Testing the Universality of Early Crustal Differentiation: A Geochemical and Geophysical Synthesis of the Jezero Crater Stratigraphy, Mars
proposals.motivation: Understanding the early crustal evolution of terrestrial planets is key to constraining their geological divergence. Pre-2026 data from the Perseverance rover at Jezero crater, Mars, reveals a compositionally stratified igneous floor sequence (Máaz and Séítah formations), providing an unprecedented opportunity to test Earth-derived models of crustal differentiation. Applying these models to Mars can reveal whether early planetary differentiation follows universal petrogenetic pathways or is fundamentally shaped by planetary parameters like size, volatile content, and tectonic style.
proposals.hypothesis: The stratified igneous lithologies of the Jezero crater floor represent a coherent, multi-stage magmatic system whose geochemical trends can be explained by established terrestrial models of fractional crystallization and crustal assimilation, implying a shared, fundamental differentiation logic for early silicate crusts.
proposals.method: We will integrate the high-resolution geochemical datasets from Perseverance (SuperCam, PIXL) for the Máaz and Séítah formations with orbital geophysical constraints. Using quantitative geochemical modeling (e.g., MELTS/alphaMELTS algorithms, EC-AFC assimilation models), we will test if the observed stratigraphy and compositions are consistent with a linked petrogenetic sequence. This will be complemented by a comparative analysis of terrestrial Archean layered intrusions and lunar ferroan anorthosite suite models to identify common and divergent differentiation signatures.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first quantitative, model-driven petrogenetic framework for the early Martian crust exposed at Jezero, moving beyond descriptive stratigraphy. It will establish a direct test case for the universality of crustal differentiation models, significantly advancing comparative planetology. The synthesis will also inform the interpretation of cached samples for future return, highlighting their value for understanding planetary evolution.
proposals.novelty: While the stratified nature of the Jezero floor is known, this proposal uniquely seeks to explain it through rigorous, quantitative terrestrial petrogenetic models, directly testing their extraterrestrial applicability. The integration of *in situ* geochemistry with geophysical discontinuity data from events like S1222a to constrain crustal structure is a novel, multi-method approach for Mars.

proposals.title: Quantifying Crustal Destruction and Recycling on Early Mars: Insights from Jezero Crater Stratigraphy and Geodynamic Modeling
proposals.motivation: On Earth, the destruction and recycling of continental crust via subduction erosion and delamination are fundamental to crustal evolution and geochemical cycling, but their rates and mechanisms remain debated. Mars, with its early, potentially plate-tectonic-like activity and preserved ancient crust, offers a crucial comparative planetological testbed. The detailed igneous stratigraphy of the Jezero crater floor, as revealed by Perseverance rover analyses, provides a unique, in-situ record of early Martian crustal processes that can inform models of crustal removal.
proposals.hypothesis: The compositional and density stratification observed within the Jezero crater floor units (Máaz, Séítah) records magmatic processes linked to a geodynamic regime capable of crustal recycling. We hypothesize that geochemical signatures and physical properties of these formations constrain the efficiency and mechanisms of crustal destruction on early Mars.
proposals.method: We will integrate geochemical data from Perseverance (SuperCam, PIXL) on the Máaz and Séítah formations with pre-2026 orbital datasets to construct a detailed petrogenetic model. This model will inform numerical simulations of early Martian mantle convection and crust-mantle interaction using the ASPECT or CitcomS geodynamic code. Specific simulations will test the viability of delamination and subduction erosion-like processes under early Martian conditions, tracking the fate of crustal material and comparing predicted magmatic outputs to the observed Jezero stratigraphy.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce the first quantitative model linking specific Martian igneous rock sequences to planetary-scale crustal recycling processes. It will provide a framework for interpreting orbital crustal composition maps in terms of geodynamic history and establish observable criteria for crustal destruction mechanisms on rocky planets beyond Earth.
proposals.novelty: While prior work has described the igneous nature of Jezero's floor, this proposal uniquely uses that detailed stratigraphy as a boundary condition to test geodynamic models of crustal removal, directly connecting rover-scale petrology to global planetary evolution. The dual-planet perspective explicitly uses Mars to test Earth-centric concepts of crustal recycling.

proposals.title: Deciphering Aeolian Stratigraphy at Victoria Crater: A Quantitative Analysis of Bounding Surfaces to Constrain Depositional and Erosional Histories
proposals.motivation: The Opportunity rover's investigation of Victoria Crater provided unprecedented, in-situ observations of Martian sedimentary stratigraphy, revealing complex cross-bedding and bounding surfaces. Distinguishing between superposition surfaces (simple bedform migration) and reactivation surfaces (erosional hiatuses within migrating bedforms) is critical for accurately reconstructing past wind regimes and environmental conditions. However, pre-2026 analyses of these outcrop-scale features have been largely qualitative, limiting robust interpretation of the crater's depositional history and its implications for regional paleoclimate.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a quantitative, morphometric analysis of bounding surface geometries and their relationships to preserved foreset laminations within the Victoria Crater stratigraphy will reveal a significant population of reactivation surfaces, indicating a dynamic aeolian environment punctuated by changes in wind direction and intensity.
proposals.method: We will apply high-resolution digital outcrop modeling techniques to the existing suite of Opportunity rover Pancam and Navcam stereo images of Victoria Crater's walls. Using structure-from-motion photogrammetry, we will construct 3D models of key stratigraphic sections. We will then implement a quantitative workflow to measure key parameters of bounding surfaces (e.g., angular discordance with overlying/underlying laminations, surface tortuosity, lateral continuity) and associated foreset beds (thickness, dip azimuth). Statistical clustering of these morphometric datasets will be used to objectively classify surfaces as either superposition or reactivation types, building on established terrestrial sedimentological frameworks.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce the first quantitative catalog of bounding surface types for a Martian sedimentary outcrop, moving beyond descriptive stratigraphy. It will provide a concrete, transferable methodology for interpreting aeolian sequences from rover imagery. The results will constrain the frequency and nature of environmental shifts recorded at Victoria Crater, offering a detailed point-comparison for orbital-scale studies of Meridiani Planum's evolution.
proposals.novelty: While bounding surfaces at Victoria have been described, this study is novel in applying quantitative terrestrial sedimentological analysis specifically to distinguish reactivation from superposition surfaces on Mars, using 3D photogrammetric models derived from legacy rover data. This approach directly tests depositional models for the Burns Formation that were previously inferred rather than measured.

proposals.title: Subsurface Architecture of the Máaz and Séítah Formations: A Synthetic Ground-Penetrating Radar Investigation of Igneous Stratigraphy in Jezero Crater, Mars
proposals.motivation: Perseverance rover observations have established the igneous nature of the Jezero crater floor, revealing compositionally and density-stratified units like the Máaz and Séítah formations. However, the subsurface geometry of contacts, internal layering, and potential intrusive relationships between these units remains poorly constrained, limiting our understanding of the volcanic processes that constructed this terrain. Applying terrestrial-style ground-penetrating radar (GPR) analysis can bridge the gap between surface geochemistry and the 3D subsurface structure.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that discrete subsurface radar reflectors, generated by contrasts in dielectric properties, will image the bounding surface between the Séítah and Máaz formations and reveal internal igneous bedding or jointing patterns not visible at the surface, providing a volumetric view of the crater floor's construction.
proposals.method: We will generate synthetic GPR profiles for the Jezero crater floor using pre-2026 methods. First, we will construct detailed 2D and 3D subsurface geologic models based on published Perseverance and orbital data, assigning electromagnetic properties (dielectric permittivity, conductivity) to units like the Séítah olivine cumulates and Máaz lavas based on their known compositions and densities. We will then use finite-difference time-domain (FDTD) modeling (e.g., gprMax open-source software) to simulate radar wave propagation at frequencies analogous to the RIMFAX instrument on the rover (e.g., 150-1200 MHz). The analysis will focus on identifying characteristic reflector geometries for different igneous contacts (e.g., interleaved vs. intrusive).
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first integrated 3D electromagnetic model of the Jezero igneous stratigraphy, directly linking surface geochemistry to subsurface architecture. It will deliver testable predictions for RIMFAX observations and establish a framework for interpreting subsurface radar reflectors in terms of specific igneous processes (e.g., lava flow stacking, sill intrusion, cumulate layering), advancing the geologic interpretation of the crater floor.
proposals.novelty: While orbital and rover-based radar studies exist, this proposal uniquely applies advanced synthetic GPR modeling, grounded in the specific mineralogy and density of Jezero units from Perseverance, to interpret subsurface igneous stratigraphy—a novel fusion of detailed geochemical constraints with quantitative near-surface geophysics.

proposals.title: Constraining Late Noachian-Early Hesperian Atmospheric Dynamics at Jezero Crater: A CFD-Based Paleowind Model for Bedform Development on the Igneous Crater Floor
proposals.motivation: The Máaz and Séítah formations, the igneous floor units of Jezero crater explored by Perseverance, exhibit wind-eroded textures and potential aeolian bedforms. Reconstructing the paleowind regimes that shaped these primary igneous landscapes is critical for understanding post-emplacement surface modification and sediment transport pathways that may have influenced the ancient lacustrine environment. This bridges the geochemical study of the crater's igneous stratigraphy with the physical processes that governed its later surficial evolution.
proposals.hypothesis: The observed erosional bedforms and wind-sculpted features on the Jezero crater floor can be reproduced by a specific, stable atmospheric circulation pattern driven by the crater's topography and regional slope during the Late Noachian-Early Hesperian. This pattern controlled initial sediment mobilization from the igneous substrate.
proposals.method: We will employ Reynolds-Averaged Navier-Stokes (RANS) computational fluid dynamics (CFD) using the OpenFOAM architecture. A high-resolution digital terrain model of Jezero and its surroundings, based on pre-2026 HRSC and CTX data, will serve as the simulation boundary. Simulations will test a range of plausible paleo-atmospheric densities (0.01-0.1 bar CO2) and wind directions. The Large-Eddy Simulation (LES) approach will be used for detailed turbulence analysis around key bedforms. Resultant wind stress fields will be input into a discrete particle model (e.g., COMSALT) to predict sediment transport and dune initiation.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first quantitative, physics-based model of paleowind flow specific to the Jezero crater floor during the era of its major igneous resurfacing. It will directly link Perseverance's in-situ observations of igneous geochemistry and texture to regional atmospheric dynamics, offering a testable mechanism for sediment source-to-sink transport prior to and during lake formation. The validated CFD framework can be applied to other Martian paleoenvironments.
proposals.novelty: While aeolian studies on Mars are common, applying high-fidelity CFD to model wind flow specifically over the complex, compositionally stratified igneous terrain of a well-characterized paleolake basin is novel. This integrates discrete igneous unit geometries from recent rover-based findings into a dynamic atmospheric model to explain their erosion and sediment contribution.

proposals.title: A Terrestrial Analog for Jezero Crater's Igneous Stratigraphy: Integrated Geologic and Geophysical Study of the Lonar Impact Crater, India
proposals.motivation: Perseverance rover data from Jezero crater reveals a compositionally and density stratified igneous floor sequence, interpreted as a volcanic construct or impact melt sheet with complex eolian overprinting. Interpreting the formation and modification of these units is critical for understanding early Martian magmatism and crater-scale sedimentary processes. A detailed, ground-truthed terrestrial analog study at a similar scale is needed to develop and test geologic models for the origin and preservation of such stratified crater fill.
proposals.hypothesis: The igneous and sedimentary stratigraphy exposed within the Lonar impact crater, India, provides a valid, process-oriented analog for the crater floor units of Jezero crater, Mars, with comparable scales of magmatic differentiation, impact-related alteration, and subsequent eolian deposition and erosion.
proposals.method: We will conduct a coordinated field, lab, and remote sensing campaign at Lonar Crater. Field mapping will document the stratigraphy, structure, and contacts of the Deccan basalt target rock, impact breccias, and post-impact lacustrine and eolian sediments. Petrographic, geochemical (XRF, ICP-MS), and mineralogical (microprobe, XRD) analyses of drilled and sampled cores will characterize igneous differentiation trends and alteration halos. High-resolution UAV-based photogrammetry and multispectral imaging will be used to construct a 3D model of the crater's interior stratigraphy, enabling direct geometric and volumetric comparison to orbital and rover-based models of Jezero's Máaz and Séítah formations.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce a rigorously documented, process-based analog model for stratified igneous crater fill, directly informing the interpretation of Perseverance's in-situ observations. It will provide a framework for distinguishing primary igneous stratigraphy from secondary sedimentary draping and erosional exhumation in crater settings. The study will yield predictive models for lithologic heterogeneity and alteration potential applicable to future Mars sample return analysis.
proposals.novelty: While Lonar is a known impact analog, no pre-2026 study has systematically investigated its entire interior stratigraphy—from basement basalt through impact products to modern eolian beds—as a unified, scaled analog for the specific igneous and aeolian sequence discovered by Perseverance at Jezero.

proposals.title: A Comparative Igneous Petrology Framework: Testing the Analogy Between Archean Subduction and Martian Crustal Formation at Jezero Crater
proposals.motivation: The assumption of uniformitarian subduction processes since the Archean is increasingly challenged by geochemical and petrological evidence for distinct tectonic regimes. Concurrently, the Perseverance rover's discovery of compositionally stratified igneous units in Jezero crater provides a unique, well-characterized analog for investigating early planetary crustal formation processes. This project leverages the Martian crust as a natural laboratory to test models of early Earth tectonics, where direct evidence is fragmentary.
proposals.hypothesis: The igneous stratigraphy and geochemical evolution of the Máaz and Séítah formations in Jezero crater record crustal formation and differentiation processes that are analogous to those operative in Archean subduction zone settings, specifically involving the interplay of mantle melting, magma ocean crystallization, and density-driven crustal stratification.
proposals.method: We will conduct a detailed comparative petrogenetic analysis using the full geochemical dataset from Perseverance (SuperCam, PIXL) for the Máaz and Séítah formations. This will involve quantitative modeling of fractional crystallization and partial melting using algorithms like MELTS/alphaMELTS to reconstruct parental magma compositions and crystallization sequences. We will compare these model outputs to geochemical proxies for Archean subduction zone magmas (e.g., high-Mg andesites, tonalite-trondhjemite-granodiorite suites) using principal component analysis and machine learning classification (e.g., random forest) to identify statistically significant compositional parallels.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a rigorous, data-driven framework for comparing planetary crustal formation styles, directly linking Martian igneous stratigraphy to terrestrial tectonic analogs. It will provide a novel test for models of non-uniformitarian Archean subduction by using a planetary-scale example of early crustal construction. The results will refine our understanding of both Martian geologic history and the range of possible tectonic processes on early Earth.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in explicitly using the pristine, in-situ igneous record from Mars as a comparative end-member for testing Earth's Archean tectonic models, moving beyond terrestrial comparisons alone. This approach leverages the detailed, spatially contextualized Perseverance dataset to interrogate the fundamental petrological processes common to both planets.

proposals.title: Constraining the Composition of the Jezero Depleted Mantle Reservoir Through Inverse Modeling of Igneous Crustal Stratigraphy
proposals.motivation: The Perseverance rover has revealed a compositionally stratified igneous crust in Jezero crater, with the Séítah unit (olivine-rich cumulate) underlying the Máaz formation (pyroxene-bearing). This stratigraphy represents a differentiated igneous sequence, providing a direct sample of early Martian crust. Inverting these crustal compositions to constrain the parent mantle source is a critical, unexploited step for understanding the geochemical evolution of the Martian mantle and the petrogenesis of its earliest crust.
proposals.hypothesis: The major and trace element compositions of the Máaz and Séítah formations can be quantitatively linked through fractional crystallization to a single, common parental magma, whose composition can be inverted to characterize the composition and degree of depletion of its mantle source region.
proposals.method: We will employ a two-step inverse modeling approach. First, using the observed mineralogy and geochemistry from SuperCam and other Perseverance instruments, we will apply least-squares mass balance and energy-constrained forward models (e.g., using the Petrolog3 software) to calculate the composition of the parental melt for the Jezero sequence. Second, we will use this calculated melt composition in a reverse batch melting model, applying a Markov Chain Monte Carlo (MCMC) algorithm to solve the inverse problem. This will constrain the major element composition and modal mineralogy of the depleted mantle source, incorporating pre-2026 estimates of Martian mantle potential temperature and oxygen fugacity.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce the first quantitatively constrained model for the composition of a specific depleted mantle reservoir on Mars, as sampled by the Jezero igneous suite. It will directly test models for large-scale mantle heterogeneity versus localized mantle melting processes early in Martian history and provide a geochemical benchmark for interpreting Martian meteorites and orbital data.
proposals.novelty: While previous studies have described the crustal stratigraphy, this proposal uniquely frames it as an inverse problem to directly quantify mantle source characteristics, moving from descriptive petrology to a quantitative geodynamic constraint. The application of probabilistic inversion techniques to *in-situ* rover data for mantle composition is a novel synthesis.

proposals.title: Constraining Early Martian Crustal Recycling: Insights from Igneous Stratigraphy and Composition at Jezero Crater
proposals.motivation: While extensive work, including from the Perseverance rover, has characterized the igneous growth and stratification of the Martian crust at Jezero crater, the complementary process of crustal destruction via recycling into the mantle remains poorly quantified. Understanding the balance between crustal growth and recycling is critical for modeling the thermal and geochemical evolution of early Mars. This study addresses a key gap by focusing on the potential mechanisms and rates of crustal recycling, the inverse of the well-studied growth processes.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that geochemical signatures within the igneous stratigraphy of the Jezero crater floor (Máaz and Séítah formations) record evidence of crustal recycling, such as assimilation of older crust or mantle source contamination by recycled materials, which can be used to place initial quantitative bounds on early Martian crustal destruction rates.
proposals.method: We will integrate the high-resolution geochemical and mineralogical datasets from Perseverance (SuperCam, PIXL) for the Máaz and Séítah formations with pre-2026 orbital data. Using quantitative geochemical modeling (e.g., mass balance assimilation-fractional crystallization calculations and trace element mantle melting models), we will search for signatures of crustal assimilation or a hybrid mantle source. We will specifically apply forward and inverse modeling algorithms to deconvolve primary mantle melts from crustal overprints, leveraging the known igneous stratigraphy and density contrasts to inform the physical plausibility of foundering or delamination events.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first quantitative framework for assessing crustal recycling processes in the specific geologic context of early Martian igneous provinces. It will directly complement existing models of crustal growth by proposing testable mechanisms and rates for its destruction, thereby advancing a more holistic understanding of early Martian crustal evolution. The findings will also inform the interpretation of returned samples from these units.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in shifting the focus from crustal formation to its destruction within the same well-characterized igneous system, applying detailed geochemical interrogation typically used for terrestrial crustal recycling studies to in-situ Martian data for the first time.

proposals.title: A Multi-Scale Stratigraphic Map of Gale Crater's Lacustrine Deposits: Integrating Orbital Hyperspectral Mapping with Rover-Based Ground Truth
proposals.motivation: The Curiosity rover has confirmed the presence of a long-lived, habitable lacustrine environment in Gale crater's Mount Sharp group. However, the full spatial extent, thickness, and stratigraphic continuity of these ancient lake deposits remain poorly constrained from orbital data alone, limiting our understanding of the paleolake's evolution, total water budget, and sedimentary architecture. A comprehensive map is essential to place the rover's detailed geochemical and sedimentological observations into a robust basin-scale context.
proposals.hypothesis: The lacustrine sedimentary strata identified by the Curiosity rover form a laterally extensive, mappable stratigraphic package that can be traced across the crater floor and lower slopes of Mount Sharp using orbital spectral data, revealing the paleolake's maximum areal extent and depositional geometry.
proposals.method: We will integrate orbital data from CRISM (Compact Reconnaissance Imaging Spectrometer for Mars) and HiRISE (High Resolution Imaging Science Experiment) with in-situ geochemical and textural data from the Curiosity rover's ChemCam, APXS, and Mastcam instruments. A supervised classification algorithm (e.g., Support Vector Machine) will be trained on CRISM spectral endmembers of lacustrine minerals (e.g., phyllosilicates, sulfates) identified along the rover traverse. This classifier will be applied to the broader CRISM scene over Gale to map the predicted spatial distribution of lacustrine facies. HiRISE DTMs will provide topographic context to delineate stratigraphic contacts and thicknesses.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce the first quantitative, mineralogically informed map of Gale's lacustrine system, defining its basin-scale stratigraphy. It will directly test depositional models (e.g., playa vs. deep lake) and provide critical context for the observed igneous-sedimentary record, linking the crater floor's igneous basement (as studied at Jezero) to the overlying sedimentary fill. The integrated rover-orbital methodology will serve as a template for interpreting other paleolake basins.
proposals.novelty: While previous studies have identified lacustrine minerals from orbit, this work uniquely uses the Curiosity rover's detailed stratigraphic column as a calibrated 'ground truth' to train and validate a basin-scale spectral-stratigraphic model, moving from point observations to a predictive three-dimensional depositional framework.

proposals.title: A Paleomagnetic Stratigraphy for the Gale Crater Lacustrine Sequence: Correlating Sedimentary Layers and Testing Martian Dynamo Timing
proposals.motivation: The sedimentary strata explored by the Curiosity rover in Gale crater provide a rich record of ancient Martian surface environments, but correlating specific lacustrine layers to a global timeline remains challenging. Establishing a high-resolution chronostratigraphic framework is essential for understanding the timing and duration of habitable conditions. Paleomagnetism, successfully used on Earth for correlation and dating, offers a novel method to address this by exploiting the record of Martian magnetic field reversals potentially preserved in these sediments.
proposals.hypothesis: The fine-grained lacustrine mudstones of the Murray formation (Gale crater) preserve a detectable and interpretable record of paleomagnetic reversals. This record can be used to construct a local magnetic polarity stratigraphy that correlates specific sedimentary layers to the broader timeline of Martian dynamo activity inferred from orbital magnetic and crater paleomagnetic studies.
proposals.method: We will analyze the existing archive of Curiosity's Mars Hand Lens Imager (MAHLI) and Alpha Particle X-Ray Spectrometer (APXS) data to identify the most compositionally homogeneous, fine-grained lacustrine targets. Using pre-2026 terrestrial paleomagnetic correlation techniques as an analog framework, we will develop a method to synthesize orbital magnetic anomaly maps over Gale with the local stratigraphy. The core approach involves constructing a synthetic magnetic polarity column for the crater floor and Mount Sharp strata, constrained by the known timing of the Martian dynamo's decline (pre-4.0 Ga), and testing its alignment with the observed remanent magnetism data from the rover's sample measurements and the observed stratigraphic sequence.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce the first detailed paleomagnetic stratigraphy for a Martian sedimentary sequence, offering a new tool for intra-crater and potentially inter-crater correlation. It will directly test models for the timing of the Martian dynamo by placing sedimentary layers within that geophysical timeline, thereby linking environmental records from Gale to a global planetary evolution context.
proposals.novelty: While paleomagnetism has been used to study Martian meteorites and orbital anomalies, its systematic application as a correlation tool for in-situ sedimentary sequences on Mars is novel. This bridges the gap between regional geophysics (InSight, orbital magnetics) and detailed sedimentology performed by rovers, a synthesis not yet attempted in pre-2026 literature.

proposals.title: Aeolian Exhumation as a Unifying Process: Quantifying Modern Gully Formation at Jezero Crater as an Analog for the Stratigraphic Architecture of Mount Sharp
proposals.motivation: The formation of Mount Sharp (Aeolis Mons) in Gale crater is hypothesized to result from large-scale aeolian exhumation of a once more extensive sedimentary deposit. Understanding the rates and patterns of this process is critical for interpreting Mars's sedimentary record and paleoenvironment. Recent Perseverance rover findings at Jezero crater, particularly the compositionally stratified Máaz and Séítah igneous units, provide a pristine, co-located setting to study modern, small-scale aeolian exhumation features (e.g., gullies, yardangs) that act as direct process analogs.
proposals.hypothesis: Modern aeolian exhumation features within Jezero crater (e.g., in the Máaz Formation floor unit) exhibit geometric and morphometric scaling relationships that, when extrapolated, are consistent with the large-scale stratigraphic architecture of Mount Sharp, supporting exhumation as a primary formation mechanism for both.
proposals.method: We will analyze high-resolution Perseverance Mastcam-Z, SuperCam RMI, and HiRISE orbital imagery to map and quantify the 3D morphology of recent exhumation gullies within the crater floor units. This will involve applying structure-from-motion photogrammetry to rover images to create local digital terrain models (DTMs) and using GIS-based algorithms (e.g., curvature analysis, relative elevation extraction) to measure gully depth-to-width ratios, slope profiles, and orientation relative to prevailing wind directions. These metrics will be statistically compared to the large-scale geometry of Mount Sharp's strata as observed by Curiosity and orbital data.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first quantitative, process-oriented link between modern Martian surface processes and a major ancient stratigraphic landmark. It will constrain models of aeolian exhumation efficiency and its role in shaping Martian geology. The findings will directly inform the interpretation of the igneous stratigraphy at Jezero being studied by Perseverance, framing it within a dynamic erosional context.
proposals.novelty: While exhumation is proposed for Mount Sharp, this study uniquely leverages the new, detailed in-situ observations of freshly exposed, stratified igneous rocks at Jezero to test the hypothesis with modern process analogs, moving from conceptual model to quantitative morphometric validation.

proposals.title: Quantitative Modeling of Lacustrine Sediment Distribution as a Function of Crater Morphometry: Scaling from Gale and Jezero to Martian Paleolake Basins
proposals.motivation: Pre-2026 rover missions to Gale and Jezero craters have revealed complex, heterogeneous lacustrine sedimentary deposits, but the extent to which their architecture is controlled by the primary crater morphology remains unclear. A systematic, quantitative model linking crater geometry (e.g., diameter, depth, rim completeness, slope profiles) to sediment infill patterns is needed to extrapolate findings from these two sites to the hundreds of other candidate paleolake basins on Mars, informing landing site selection and paleoenvironmental interpretation.
proposals.hypothesis: The three-dimensional geometry of an impact crater (size, depth-to-diameter ratio, and rim integrity) exerts a first-order control on the spatial distribution, thickness, and stratigraphic connectivity of lacustrine sedimentary facies by governing sediment transport pathways, wave energy, and accommodation space.
proposals.method: We will compile a geomorphometric database of Martian crater basins with inferred paleolakes, using MOLA and HRSC data to extract key parameters (diameter, depth, wall slopes, breach locations). Using the well-constrained sedimentary geometries of Gale (from Curiosity) and Jezero (from Perseverance and orbital data) as end-member calibration points, we will develop a 3D geometric forward model. This model will simulate sediment infill via a reduced-complexity cellular automaton or diffusion-based algorithm, where transport rules for deltaic, lacustrine floor, and marginal facies are parameterized by crater shape, inlet positions, and assumed water levels.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce a predictive framework for sediment distribution in crater lakes, moving beyond qualitative analogy. It will provide testable predictions for the stratigraphy of unexplored basins and a quantitative context for interpreting the depositional environments at Jezero and Gale. The model will also help assess the preservation potential of specific biosignature-hosting facies across different crater types.
proposals.novelty: While individual crater lakes have been studied, a systematic, physics-informed scaling model linking generic crater morphometry to detailed sedimentary architecture has not been developed. This approach integrates the new, ground-truthed igneous and sedimentary constraints from Perseverance with broader orbital geomorphology.

proposals.title: A Terrestrial Anchor for Martian Igneous History: Hf-O Isotopes in Detrital Zircons to Constrain Early Crustal Evolution Models
proposals.motivation: Understanding early planetary crustal evolution is a fundamental goal in planetary science. On Mars, orbital data and in-situ analyses by the Perseverance rover at Jezero crater reveal a complex, stratified igneous crust, but its formation and modification history remain poorly quantified. Applying terrestrial geochemical tools to Martian-relevant terrestrial analogs and ancient zircons can provide critical ground truth for interpreting Martian lithologies and testing models of crustal growth and reworking on both planets.
proposals.hypothesis: The coupled Hf and O isotopic compositions of ancient (>3.5 Ga) detrital zircons from terrestrial cratons record distinct, quantifiable pathways of crustal growth (juvenile addition) versus reworking (recycling of older crust), providing an empirical framework to deconvolve the igneous stratigraphy and crustal discontinuities observed on Mars.
proposals.method: We will analyze Hf (by LA-MC-ICP-MS) and O (by SIMS) isotopes in a targeted suite of >3.5 Ga detrital zircons from terrains like the Jack Hills. These data will be used to construct time-series Hf-O isotopic evolution models, quantifying the proportion of juvenile vs. reworked crust through time using pre-2026 mixing and crustal evolution algorithms (e.g., two-stage Hf model age calculations and εHf-δ18O vector analysis). This terrestrial 'calibration' will then be applied as an interpretive framework to the geochemical and mineralogical data from the Máaz and Séítah formations returned by Perseverance, using known elemental proxies for crustal evolution (e.g., K/Th, Si).
proposals.contribution: This work will produce a refined, isotopically-constrained model for early terrestrial crustal evolution, directly linking zircon archives to bulk crust processes. It will provide a novel geochemical 'ruler' with which to assess the igneous stratigraphy of Jezero crater, offering specific testable predictions for the degree of crustal reworking versus juvenile magmatism in early Martian crustal blocks.
proposals.novelty: While detrital zircon Hf-O studies are common for Earth, their explicit use as a quantitative analog framework to interpret in-situ igneous stratigraphy on another planet (Mars) is novel. This bridges the disciplinary gap between terrestrial geochronology/isotope geochemistry and the current, lithology-focused exploration of Martian igneous terrains.

proposals.title: Constraining Archean Mantle Potential Temperature and Source Heterogeneity via Inverse Geochemical Modeling of Global Komatiite and Basalt Compositions
proposals.motivation: Understanding the thermal and compositional evolution of the early Earth's mantle is fundamental to planetary differentiation models. Komatiites and associated basalts provide the primary geochemical record of deep mantle melting in the Archean, but quantitative estimates of mantle potential temperature (Tp) and source composition from these rocks remain contentious and model-dependent. This work directly addresses this gap by applying rigorous inverse methods to a global geochemical dataset.
proposals.hypothesis: Archean mantle melting can be explained by a limited range of potential temperatures (Tp) and a small number of distinct, yet globally distributed, mantle source reservoirs. We hypothesize that inverse modeling will reveal Tp estimates lower than some previous petrological studies and will identify specific source heterogeneities related to early crust formation.
proposals.method: We will compile a global database of high-quality major and trace element compositions for Archean komatiites and coeval basalts. The inverse model will use the pMELTS algorithm to simulate polybaric, fractional melting of a parameterized mantle source. A Markov Chain Monte Carlo (MCMC) sampling approach will be employed to explore the multi-dimensional parameter space (e.g., initial Tp, pressure of melting, source composition). The best-fit models will be those that minimize the misfit between simulated and observed lava compositions for each suite.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide statistically robust, quantitative constraints on Archean mantle Tp and source compositions, reducing the uncertainty in early Earth thermal models. It will establish a formalized geochemical framework for interpreting igneous crustal sequences on other terrestrial planets, such as the ancient crust of Mars being investigated by the Perseverance rover in Jezero crater.
proposals.novelty: While forward modeling of komatiites is common, this proposal applies a formal Bayesian inverse approach to the problem, simultaneously solving for temperature, pressure, and source composition. This method directly integrates the analytical and planetary geology expertise from the researcher's work on Martian igneous terrains into a deep-time Earth science question.

proposals.title: Constraining Early Continental Volume Through a Global Mass Balance Model of Archean Sedimentary Flux
proposals.motivation: The volume and growth rate of continental crust in the Archean remain poorly constrained, with major implications for early Earth's geodynamics, climate, and habitability. Pre-2026 studies of preserved cratons and detrital zircons provide local snapshots, but a quantitative global synthesis is lacking. This gap can be addressed by leveraging the well-established link between continental freeboard, erosion, and the sedimentary record, a principle central to McLennan's prior work on Martian sedimentary systems at Jezero crater.
proposals.hypothesis: The global mass of preserved Archean sedimentary rocks, when corrected for estimated preservation and recycling rates, provides a robust first-order constraint on the total volume of emergent continental crust that existed during the Archean Eon.
proposals.method: We will compile a global geospatial database of Archean sedimentary rock units, their lithologies, and estimated masses from pre-2026 geologic maps and literature. A Monte Carlo simulation framework will be developed to model the mass balance, incorporating probability distributions for key unknowns: the original sediment production flux from continental weathering, the fraction of sediment deposited in preservable basins (e.g., cratonic interiors vs. lost to subduction), and post-depositional recycling rates. The model will be iteratively tuned to match the observed preserved mass, outputting a constrained probability distribution for total emergent continental area/volume through the Archean.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce the first quantitatively rigorous, observationally anchored estimate of global Archean continental volume, moving beyond qualitative growth curves. It will provide a critical boundary condition for models of crustal generation and destruction mechanisms in the early Earth. The methodology establishes a terrestrial analog framework that could later be applied to early Martian crustal evolution, linking to findings from the Perseverance rover.
proposals.novelty: While sedimentary mass balance is a classic concept, its application at a global scale to the poorly preserved Archean record using modern probabilistic modeling is novel. The approach directly connects the empirical rock record to crustal volume, bypassing reliance on more speculative geochemical or isotopic proxies alone.

proposals.title: A Provenance and Alteration Model for the Bagnold Dunes, Jezero Crater: Integrating Perseverance Rover Data to Constrain Source-to-Sink Transport History
proposals.motivation: The active Bagnold Dunes in Jezero crater represent a critical, yet underutilized, archive of Martian surface processes. Their composition integrates material from across the crater floor and beyond, including the igneous Máaz and Séítah formations. Reconstructing the transport and alteration history of dune grains is essential for understanding sediment cycling, the chemical weathering environment, and identifying source lithologies not directly sampled by the Perseverance rover.
proposals.hypothesis: The mineralogical and chemical diversity of Bagnold Dune grains, as measured by Perseverance instruments, can be quantitatively modeled to reveal discrete source regions (e.g., Máaz vs. Séítah) and constrain the relative roles of physical comminution versus aqueous alteration during transport and residence.
proposals.method: We will compile and integrate mineralogical data from SuperCam (LIBS, Raman, VISIR) and PIXL, and chemical data from SuperCam LIBS, from Perseverance's observations of the Bagnold Dunes and known bedrock units. A statistical unmixing model (e.g., a non-negative matrix factorization algorithm) will be applied to the dune grain dataset to identify endmember compositions. These endmembers will be compared to the characterized bedrock units. We will then apply a forward geochemical transport-alteration model, using pre-2026 Martian alteration frameworks, to test pathways from candidate source compositions to the observed dune grain chemistries.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce the first quantitative provenance model for an active dune field on Mars, directly linking modern sediments to their bedrock sources. It will place novel constraints on the intensity and nature of subaerial alteration in Jezero crater post-dating the main igneous and lacustrine activity, informing models of Mars's late-stage aqueous history.
proposals.novelty: While the igneous stratigraphy of Jezero's floor is being characterized, this proposal uniquely focuses on the *derived sediments*, applying compositional unmixing and alteration modeling specifically to rover-scale, in-situ dune grain data to solve a dynamic transport history problem.

proposals.title: Spatial Heterogeneity of Mafic and Olivine-Rich Sands in the Bagnold Dune Field, Jezero Crater: Implications for Provenance and Crustal Stratigraphy
proposals.motivation: The Bagnold Dune Field is a dynamic sedimentary system on the floor of Jezero crater, acting as a natural sampler of the local and regional igneous crust. Pre-2026 orbital and rover-based analyses (e.g., by Curiosity at Gale) show dune sands can be compositionally distinct from immediate bedrock, offering a window into broader crustal diversity. Systematic, in-situ characterization of its mineralogical variability has not been conducted, limiting our understanding of sediment sources and the compositional stratification of the igneous crust revealed by the Máaz and Séítah formations.
proposals.hypothesis: The mineralogy of the Bagnold sands exhibits systematic spatial heterogeneity that correlates with dune morphology (e.g., crest vs. slip face) and geographic location, reflecting a mixture of source lithologies from both the crater floor igneous units and distal mafic materials.
proposals.method: We will utilize the Perseverance rover's SuperCam and PIXL instruments to conduct a spatially resolved survey of multiple dune targets across the accessible Bagnold field. At each site, we will collect LIBS spectra for bulk elemental chemistry and VNIR reflectance spectra for mineral identification (focusing on olivine, pyroxene, and plagioclase signatures). We will employ established pre-2026 spectral unmixing algorithms (e.g., Modified Gaussian Model for VNIR, multivariate analysis for LIBS) to quantify mineral abundances.
