These point measurements will be integrated with Mastcam-Z multispectral observations and rover traverse context to map compositional trends.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce the first high-resolution, ground-truthed map of active dune composition within Jezero crater. It will directly constrain the provenance of aeolian sediments and test models for the contribution of the local stratified igneous terrain (Máaz, Séítah) versus regional sources. The findings will inform models of martian crustal evolution and sediment transport.
proposals.novelty: While previous studies identified the bulk mafic nature of the dunes, this systematic, multi-instrument spatial survey to quantify heterogeneity and link it to specific igneous units (Máaz vs. Séítah) and transport processes is novel. It leverages Perseverance's unique capability to correlate in-situ dune mineralogy with detailed crater floor geology.

proposals.title: Provenance and Crustal Evolution of the Jezero Crater Region from Detrital Zircon Geochronology of Modern Aeolian Sands
proposals.motivation: The Perseverance rover has confirmed the igneous nature of the Máaz and Séítah formations, revealing a compositionally stratified crustal section within Jezero crater. However, the broader provenance and crustal evolution of the source terrains supplying sediment to the crater floor and delta remain poorly constrained. Applying terrestrial sedimentological techniques to martian aeolian sands can provide critical insights into the regional geologic history beyond the rover's immediate traverse.
proposals.hypothesis: Detrital zircon grains, if present within Jezero's modern dune sands, will yield U-Pb age populations that record discrete magmatic events in the source region, constraining the provenance of sediments and providing a maximum depositional age for the sedimentary units they were ultimately derived from.
proposals.method: We will utilize the Scanning Habitable Environments with Raman & Luminescence for Organics & Chemicals (SHERLOC) instrument's deep-UV Raman and fluorescence spectroscopy capabilities to systematically search for zircon grains within abraded patches on aeolian dune targets, leveraging zircon's characteristic Raman peak at ~1008 cm⁻¹. Suspect grains will be further characterized with Perseverance's Planetary Instrument for X-ray Lithochemistry (PIXL) to confirm high Zr and Si content. For any identified zircons, we will employ a novel, mission-adapted spatial deconvolution analysis of PIXL X-ray fluorescence maps to estimate U, Th, and Pb concentrations, enabling preliminary model age calculations to prioritize samples for eventual return.
proposals.contribution: This work would provide the first direct geochronology of detrital minerals on Mars, offering a novel constraint on the timing of igneous events in the Jezero catchment. It would test models for the formation of the stratified igneous crust and directly inform the selection of the most scientifically valuable samples for Mars Sample Return, specifically those containing ancient zircons that could date early Martian magmatism.
proposals.novelty: While the igneous stratigraphy has been studied in situ, this proposal applies a classic terrestrial provenance method—detrital zircon geochemistry—to active martian sediments for the first time, using existing rover instruments in a new way to seek grains that are rare but potentially information-rich.

proposals.title: Inverse Sedimentary Provenance Modeling of Jezero Crater: Constraining Igneous Bedrock Diversity from Perseverance Rover Dune Geochemistry
proposals.motivation: The Perseverance rover has identified compositionally distinct igneous units (e.g., Máaz, Séítah) on the Jezero crater floor, representing a complex magmatic history. Aeolian dunes within the crater are natural samplers of this lithological diversity, but the quantitative link between dune composition and the proportions of its contributing bedrock sources remains unconstrained. This inverse problem is critical for using orbital spectral data of dunes to extrapolate local rover findings to regional geology, especially for inaccessible units.
proposals.hypothesis: The bulk mineralogy and major/trace element chemistry of an aeolian dune can be modeled as a linear mixture of characterized igneous endmembers within its catchment, allowing for the prediction of source rock types and their relative contributions to the sediment budget.
proposals.method: We will use SuperCam and PIXL data from Perseverance to define the mineralogical and chemical composition of the Jezero floor units (Máaz, Séítah) as endmembers. Dune targets (e.g., 'Séítah' dune) will be analyzed similarly. We will employ a Bayesian linear unmixing algorithm, incorporating uncertainties from instrument precision and natural variability, to solve for the proportional contribution of each endmember to the dune composition. The model will be validated by comparing predicted source contributions with geological context and transport pathways inferred from HiRISE imagery.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce the first quantitative provenance model for martian aeolian sediments directly tied to in-situ igneous rock analyses. It will test the hypothesis of a stratified igneous crust at Jezero by assessing if dune compositions require contributions from un-sampled units. The methodology will provide a framework for using orbital dune spectroscopy to map subsurface lithology elsewhere on Mars.
proposals.novelty: While mixing models are common in planetary remote sensing, applying a rigorous inverse model to in-situ dune geochemistry to solve for specific, characterized bedrock sources is novel. This directly leverages the unique ground-truth provided by Perseverance to calibrate a powerful regional exploration tool.

proposals.title: Re-evaluating Early Crustal Dynamics: A Geochemical and Petrological Model for Crustal Recycling in the Archean Earth and Mars
proposals.motivation: The canonical model of net crustal growth in the Archean is challenged by emerging evidence for significant crustal recycling, yet quantitative models of this process are lacking. Insights from recent Mars missions, particularly the detailed igneous stratigraphy and compositional layering observed by Perseverance at Jezero crater, provide a novel, well-constrained analog for investigating early planetary crustal evolution. This work leverages these extraterrestrial data to test a revised paradigm for early Earth.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the early Archean crust was in a dynamic equilibrium, where high rates of crustal production were balanced by efficient recycling via subduction, delamination, and impact erosion, rather than experiencing simple net growth. The igneous diversity and stratification at Jezero crater reflect analogous processes.
proposals.method: We will develop a coupled geochemical-petrological box model, incorporating mantle melting, crustal differentiation, and recycling fluxes. The model will be constrained by pre-2026 terrestrial Archean rock compositions (e.g., tonalite-trondhjemite-granodiorite suites) and the newly defined igneous stratigraphy of the Jezero crater floor (Máaz and Séítah formations). Inverse Monte Carlo algorithms will be used to fit the model to the observed geochemical arrays, solving for the rates of crustal production and recycling required to explain the data.
proposals.contribution: This research will produce the first quantitative model integrating Martian igneous stratigraphy to test Archean crustal recycling scenarios. It will provide a framework to reinterpret the terrestrial Archean rock record, moving beyond growth-only models. The findings will impact our understanding of early planetary thermal evolution and the geodynamic regime of the Hadean/Archean Earth.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in directly using the detailed, spatially resolved igneous stratigraphy from another planet (Mars/Jezero) as a key constraint for modeling early Earth geodynamics—an approach made possible only by post-2020 mission data. It explicitly tests the equilibrium hypothesis against the canonical growth model using modern inverse methods.

proposals.title: Geochemical Fingerprinting of Martian Lower Crustal Protoliths: A Comparative Study of Granulite Xenoliths and Jezero Crater Igneous Stratigraphy
proposals.motivation: The composition and formation of the Martian lower crust remain poorly constrained, limiting our understanding of planetary differentiation and early magmatic processes. Recent findings from the Perseverance rover at Jezero crater reveal a stratified igneous crust, while seismic data from InSight suggests a potentially layered shallow crust. Determining the protoliths of lower crustal granulites, sampled as xenoliths in Martian meteorites, is critical for connecting these disparate datasets and building a unified model of crustal evolution.
proposals.hypothesis: The protoliths of Martian granulite xenoliths are compositionally distinct from the surface igneous units observed at Jezero crater, representing either earlier crustal formation events or deep-seated cumulates from evolved magmatic systems. These protoliths can be identified through immobile element ratios and radiogenic isotope systematics.
proposals.method: We will perform high-precision in-situ geochemical analysis (major/trace elements via EMPA and LA-ICP-MS) on a suite of Martian granulite xenoliths from meteorites like NWA 7034 and its brecciated relatives. Key immobile trace element ratios (e.g., Nb/Ta, Zr/Hf, Th/Nb) and rare earth element patterns will be used to discriminate between potential protoliths (mafic cumulates, basaltic lavas, intrusive rocks). This geochemical 'fingerprinting' will be systematically compared to the igneous stratigraphy defined for the Máaz and Séítah formations by Perseverance, using the pre-2026 SuperCam and PIXL datasets to establish compositional fields for known surface units.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first direct geochemical link between Martian meteorite samples of the deep crust and in-situ observed surface geology. It will test models of a compositionally stratified crust and constrain the petrogenetic relationship, or lack thereof, between the ancient lower crust and younger surface volcanics. The results will significantly advance the interpretation of global geophysical models of crustal structure.
proposals.novelty: While granulite xenoliths have been studied petrographically, a systematic geochemical campaign to identify their protoliths and explicitly compare them to the definitive igneous stratigraphy now available from Jezero crater has not been conducted. This leverages the new, ground-truthed context from Mars 2020 to interpret the meteoritic record.

proposals.title: Quantifying Ancient Erosion and Upper Crustal Composition at Jezero Crater, Mars, Through Integrated Sedimentary Basin and Igneous Stratigraphy Modeling
proposals.motivation: Pre-2026 data from the Perseverance rover has established the igneous nature and compositional stratification of the crater floor units (Máaz, Séítah). However, the processes and rates of early Martian crustal evolution, including the erosion of these primary igneous terrains to produce the observed sedimentary delta, remain poorly quantified. Applying terrestrial basin analysis techniques to Mars provides a novel framework to link igneous protolith composition, erosional denudation, and sedimentary output.
proposals.hypothesis: The volume and composition of clastic sediments in Jezero's delta can be used, via mass-balance inverse modeling, to estimate the thickness and bulk composition of eroded igneous crust, thereby constraining early Martian surface processes and upper crustal evolution.
proposals.method: We will first construct a high-resolution 3D geologic model of Jezero's igneous floor using Perseverance SuperCam, Mastcam-Z, and orbital data to define the volume and composition of the Máaz and Séítah formations. This source model will be integrated with a sedimentary basin analysis model (using 3D backstripping and decompaction algorithms) of the deltaic stratigraphy observed in the western crater. A deterministic mass-balance inversion, employing a least-squares mixing algorithm, will be used to find the best-fit eroded source composition and thickness required to generate the delta's observed sediment volume and mineralogy (e.g., olivine, pyroxene, feldspar abundances).
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first quantitative estimate of erosional denudation and crustal recycling at a specific site on early Mars, directly linking igneous crust formation with sedimentary basin formation. It will yield a testable, compositionally-defined model for the local Martian upper crust and establish a methodology to extrapolate this approach to other ancient Martian basins.
proposals.novelty: While basin analysis is standard for terrestrial petroleum systems, its application to Mars has been largely qualitative. This study is novel in its quantitative integration of *in situ* igneous stratigraphy with deltaic sedimentology to solve for paleo-erosion, moving beyond simple stratigraphic description to a process-based, mass-constrained model of crustal evolution.

proposals.title: Constraining the Volume and Composition of the Martian Crust Using Cosmochemical Abundance Models and In Situ Igneous Geochemistry from Jezero Crater
proposals.motivation: Pre-2026 geophysical and orbital data provide conflicting estimates of Martian crustal thickness and volume, hindering models of planetary differentiation and thermal evolution. The Perseverance rover's detailed geochemical analysis of the igneous Máaz and Séítah formations in Jezero crater provides a unique, ground-truthed compositional anchor point for the ancient Martian crust. This work will leverage these new in situ data within a rigorous geochemical framework to produce a more robust estimate of total crustal extraction.
proposals.hypothesis: The bulk composition of the Martian crust can be estimated by applying mass balance calculations, anchored by the geochemistry of Jezero's igneous rocks, to a model of the bulk silicate Mars derived from chondritic abundances. This will yield a constrained total crustal volume and a testable composition for the complementary depleted mantle.
proposals.method: We will first construct a bulk silicate Mars (BSM) composition using CI chondrite abundances, adjusted for volatile depletion and core formation based on pre-2026 Martian meteorite and mission data. The mean composition of the Jezero igneous crust, weighted by the stratigraphic relationships defined by Perseverance, will serve as the crustal endmember. Using a non-linear least squares inversion algorithm, we will solve for the mass fraction of crust extracted from the BSM to produce the observed crustal composition, simultaneously calculating the composition of the residual mantle. The model will be validated by comparing the predicted residual mantle composition to the geochemistry of young Martian basalts and shergottites.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide a geochemically grounded estimate of the total mass and major-element composition of the Martian crust, directly linking local rover measurements to global planetary differentiation. It will produce a self-consistent crust-mantle mass balance model that can be tested by future seismic and sample-return data. The work will also define the composition of the depleted Martian mantle, informing models of later magmatism.
proposals.novelty: While bulk silicate Earth models are mature, their application to Mars has been generic. This proposal uniquely integrates the first detailed, in situ igneous stratigraphy from another planet (Jezero crater) as a critical compositional constraint within the cosmochemical framework, moving beyond orbital inferences.

proposals.title: A Comparative Igneous Stratigraphy Approach to Earth's Oceanic Crust and Jezero Crater's Igneous Floor: Insights into Crustal Formation and Recycling
proposals.motivation: The Perseverance rover's discovery of compositionally stratified igneous units in Jezero crater provides a novel, in-situ record of magmatic processes on Mars. On Earth, the formation and recycling of oceanic crust through subduction is a primary driver of planetary differentiation and geochemical cycles, but direct observation of its full stratigraphic complexity is limited. Applying the detailed compositional mapping techniques developed for Martian igneous terrains to Earth's oceanic crust offers a powerful comparative planetology framework to understand fundamental crustal evolution mechanisms.
proposals.hypothesis: The igneous stratigraphy of the Jezero crater floor, characterized by the Máaz and Séítah formations, records magmatic differentiation processes analogous to those forming the layered igneous sequences of oceanic crust, and comparative analysis will reveal universal and environment-specific controls on crustal construction.
proposals.method: We will integrate three pre-2026 datasets and methods: 1) The SuperCam and other instrument geochemistry from Perseverance for the Máaz and Séítah formations, using the same fractional crystallization and magma source modeling applied in recent Jezero studies. 2) Geochemical data from IODP drill cores and ophiolite studies of Earth's oceanic crust, constructing analogous differentiation sequences. 3) A comparative statistical analysis, employing principal component analysis and quantitative stratigraphic modeling, to test for congruences in compositional trends, density stratification, and inferred cooling histories between the Martian and terrestrial sequences.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a direct comparative framework between the best-characterized igneous stratigraphy on another planet and Earth's oceanic crust, refining models for magma chamber processes in different gravitational and volatile environments. It will provide a novel planetary context for interpreting oceanic crustal recycling's role in long-term geochemical evolution and identify diagnostic signatures for differentiating planetary crustal types from limited remote or sample data.
proposals.novelty: While oceanic crust and Martian igneous units are studied in isolation, this proposal systematically applies the high-resolution, stratigraphically constrained analytical paradigm from the Perseverance mission to the terrestrial oceanic crustal cycle, creating a two-planet test for igneous differentiation models.

proposals.title: Extending the Igneous Record of Jezero Crater: Constraining Late Cenozoic Martian Climatic and Tectonic Transitions via In-Situ Geochemistry and Clay Mineralogy
proposals.motivation: The Perseverance rover's analysis of the Máaz and Séítah formations has revealed a compositionally stratified igneous floor sequence at Jezero crater, providing a crucial but temporally limited (~4.8 Ma) record of early Martian magmatism and aqueous alteration. Extending this geochemical and mineralogical record deeper into the late Cenozoic period is critical for understanding the timing and drivers of major transitions in Martian climate (from wetter to drier) and crustal dynamics, which are poorly constrained by orbital data alone.
proposals.hypothesis: Older, stratigraphically lower igneous units within Jezero crater's floor sequence preserve distinct clay mineral assemblages and geochemical signatures that record earlier episodes of aqueous alteration and magmatic evolution, directly linked to planetary-scale tectonic and climatic shifts prior to 4.8 Ma.
proposals.method: We will integrate high-resolution mapping from Mastcam-Z and SuperCam RMI to identify and prioritize in-situ targets on lowermost floor units, extending below the currently studied Máaz/Séítah contact. We will employ SuperCam LIBS and VISIR spectroscopy, PIXL, and SHERLOC on these targets to acquire bulk geochemistry, mineral maps, and specific clay mineral identifications (e.g., smectite, chlorite). Data will be analyzed using established pre-2026 methodologies: fractional crystallization and assimilation models for igneous petrogenesis, and thermodynamic codes (e.g., PHREEQC) to constrain paleo-fluid conditions from alteration mineralogy.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce the first in-situ, temporally extended record of magmatic and aqueous processes in Jezero crater, directly testing models for the evolution of the Martian crust and climate in the late Cenozoic. It will establish a foundational stratigraphic and geochemical context for the returned samples from these deeper units, significantly enhancing their future interpretive value.
proposals.novelty: While previous work defined the igneous nature of the upper stratigraphy, this proposal systematically targets the older, uncharacterized basement of the crater floor to directly link mineralogical alteration states to a longer timeline of planetary evolution, moving from a static snapshot to a dynamic history.

proposals.title: Assessing Post-Depositional Alteration Fronts as the Primary Control on Vertical Mineral Stratigraphy in the Jezero Crater Floor Sequence
proposals.motivation: The Perseverance rover has identified compositionally stratified igneous units in Jezero crater, notably the Máaz and Séítah formations. Pre-2026 interpretations often attribute vertical mineralogical changes to primary magmatic processes or discrete depositional events. However, the potential for post-depositional, water-rock interaction to create mineralogical fronts that mimic depositional stratigraphy has not been rigorously tested, which is critical for accurately reconstructing Mars's aqueous and magmatic history.
proposals.hypothesis: The observed vertical mineralogical changes within the Jezero crater floor sequence are not primarily depositional but are instead the result of spatially and temporally discrete fluid alteration fronts moving through initially more homogeneous igneous bedrock.
proposals.method: We will integrate geochemical and mineralogical data from Perseverance (SuperCam, PIXL) with terrestrial analog studies of subaerial and subaqueous basaltic lava flows. Using quantitative geochemical modeling (e.g., PHREEQC, Geochemist's Workbench), we will simulate isochemical and open-system alteration scenarios to predict mineral zonation patterns. We will specifically test if the stratigraphic contacts (e.g., Máaz/Séítah) and internal variations can be reproduced by models of downward-percolating, chemically evolving fluids reacting with olivine-rich and pyroxene-rich protoliths, comparing model outputs to the observed mineralogy and chemistry.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a new framework for distinguishing primary igneous stratigraphy from secondary alteration overprints in Martian crater floor contexts. It will directly inform the interpretation of Perseverance's cached samples and constrain the timing, scale, and chemistry of aqueous alteration events in Jezero crater, with implications for past habitability.
proposals.novelty: While alteration is acknowledged, existing pre-2026 models for Jezero's floor primarily interpret stratigraphy as depositional. This proposal explicitly inverts the paradigm, testing if the entire vertical sequence is an alteration artifact, a concept applied to terrestrial weathered profiles but not systematically to Mars rover-scale stratigraphy.

proposals.title: Geochemical Modeling of Evaporative Brines to Test a Kieserite-Formation Hypothesis for the Top of the Burns Formation, Meridiani Planum, Mars
proposals.motivation: The sulfate-rich Burns Formation at Meridiani Planum, explored by the Opportunity rover, is a key record of ancient acidic and saline surface waters on Mars. The mineralogical sequence, particularly the hypothesized presence of kieserite (MgSO4·H2O) at the top of the section, is critical for constraining the evolving chemistry, temperature, and evaporation history of late-stage fluids in this paleo-environment. Testing this hypothesis directly addresses the diagenetic and depositional processes that shaped one of Mars's most extensive sedimentary records.
proposals.hypothesis: The observed mineralogical assemblage at the uppermost Burns Formation, specifically the potential kieserite signature, can be reproduced by geochemical models of evaporative concentration from late-stage, low-pH brines derived from the alteration of olivine-rich basalts under Martian atmospheric conditions.
proposals.method: We will employ the Geochemist's Workbench (GWB) software suite, utilizing its thermodynamic database (updated with relevant Mg-Ca-Na-K-Fe-S-Cl brine and mineral data pre-2026) to model evaporation pathways. Starting fluid compositions will be constrained by published Opportunity APXS data from the Burns Formation and experimental leachates of Martian basalt analogs. We will run equilibrium evaporation models at relevant temperatures (0-25°C) and P_CO2, tracking mineral precipitation sequences, with sensitivity analyses on key parameters like initial Fe/Mg ratio and oxidation state to test the stability field of kieserite versus other Mg-sulfates like epsomite or hexahydrite.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a quantitative framework for interpreting the terminal evaporative conditions at Meridiani Planum, either supporting or refuting the kieserite hypothesis. It will refine our understanding of late-stage brine chemistry on early Mars and offer a diagnostic geochemical model applicable to other sulfate-bearing sequences, such as those observed from orbit in Gale and Jezero craters.
proposals.novelty: While Burns Formation geochemistry has been modeled broadly, this proposal focuses specifically on testing the formation of a single, diagnostically important mineral (kieserite) at the stratigraphic cap, linking its presence or absence to specific, quantifiable evaporative endpoints. This targeted approach using up-to-date brine chemistry models was not fully explored in initial post-Opportunity studies.

proposals.title: Quantitative Diagenetic Modeling of Martian Sulfate Formation: Applying Terrestrial Playa Geochemical Models to the Burns Formation at Meridiani Planum
proposals.motivation: The sulfate-rich Burns Formation at Meridiani Planum, investigated by the Opportunity rover, is a key record of ancient acidic and saline surface waters on Mars, but its detailed diagenetic history remains poorly constrained. Pre-2026 terrestrial studies of evaporative playas (e.g., Bristol Dry Lake, Salar de Atacama) have developed sophisticated quantitative models for mineral transitions (e.g., gypsum-anhydrite-bassanite) driven by fluctuating water activity and brine chemistry. Applying these well-calibrated Earth models to Mars provides a novel pathway to decode the paleoenvironmental conditions and diagenetic intensity recorded in Martian sedimentary rocks.
proposals.hypothesis: The mineralogy and stratigraphy of the Burns Formation can be accurately reproduced by applying thermodynamic and kinetic diagenetic models developed for terrestrial evaporitic playas, constraining the water-to-rock ratios, brine compositions, and evaporation cycles prevalent in early Martian groundwater systems.
proposals.method: We will adapt established geochemical reaction-path models (e.g., using PHREEQC or Geochemist's Workbench) that simulate evaporation and diagenesis in terrestrial playas. The models will be initialized with pre-2026 Mars-relevant fluid compositions derived from MER Opportunity data and orbital detections, incorporating known mineral phases (jarosite, hematite, Mg/Ca-sulfates). We will run simulations under a range of P-T conditions (martian surface, shallow subsurface) and evaporation pathways, quantitatively comparing the predicted mineral sequences and abundances to the observed stratigraphy and mineralogy of the Burns Formation.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first quantitative, process-based diagenetic model for the Burns Formation, moving beyond qualitative interpretations. It will deliver specific constraints on the chemical and hydrological regimes of early Martian groundwater. The adapted model framework will also serve as a new tool for interpreting sulfate-rich strata observed elsewhere on Mars, including from orbital spectrometers.
proposals.novelty: While Martian sulfates have long been compared to terrestrial evaporites, this study is novel in its direct application of quantitative, code-based diagenetic models from Earth playas to a specific Martian formation, integrating known igneous crustal compositions from researchers like McLennan to define starting brine chemistry.

proposals.title: Microscale Textures and Paragenesis of Sulfates in Igneous Terrain: Experimental Constraints and Rover-Scale Observations for Jezero Crater, Mars
proposals.motivation: The Perseverance rover has identified sulfate minerals within the compositionally stratified Máaz and Séítah igneous formations at Jezero crater. These sulfates are key indicators of post-magmatic aqueous alteration, but their formation timing and environmental conditions (e.g., late-stage igneous fluids vs. later surface groundwater) remain ambiguous. Understanding their microscale crystallization history is critical for deconvolving the complex, potentially multi-phase aqueous history recorded in Jezero's volcanic floor.
proposals.hypothesis: The crystal morphology, intra-grain chemical zoning, and spatial association of sulfate phases with primary igneous and alteration minerals, as observed at the microscale, encode diagnostic information about the temperature, pH, and fluid-to-rock ratio of the altering fluids, distinguishing between endogenic hydrothermal and exogenic meteoric processes.
proposals.method: We will integrate laboratory experiments with micro-analytical techniques and rover instrument simulation. 1) Hydrothermal batch experiments will synthesize sulfates (e.g., Ca-, Mg-, Fe-sulfates) from Jezero-relevant basaltic compositions under controlled T, pH, and salinity. 2) Experimental products will be analyzed using SEM-EDS, electron microprobe, and Raman spectroscopy to establish diagnostic textural-compositional fingerprints. 3) These fingerprints will guide a systematic re-analysis of Perseverance SuperCam (LIBS, Raman) and PIXL micro-XRF data, using supervised classification algorithms (e.g., Random Forest) trained on experimental spectra to map sulfate phases and infer paragenetic sequences in situ.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce a calibrated framework for interpreting sulfate formation conditions from micro-textures and chemistry observable by rover payloads. It will directly constrain the alteration environment of Jezero's crater floor, testing models of its aqueous history. The experimental protocols and analytical workflows will establish a new methodology for interpreting microscale mineralogy in planetary remote sensing.
proposals.novelty: While sulfates on Mars have been studied remotely and in meteorites, this proposal uniquely bridges controlled laboratory simulation of sulfate crystallization in igneous contexts with targeted re-interpretation of *in situ* rover micro-analytical data, moving beyond bulk detection to process-oriented paragenetic analysis.

proposals.title: Inverting Surface Sulfate Assemblages at Jezero Crater to Model Subsurface Brine Composition and Evolution
proposals.motivation: The Perseverance rover has identified diverse sulfate minerals (e.g., Ca-, Mg-, Fe-sulfates) within the sedimentary and altered igneous strata of Jezero crater. These evaporative minerals are direct precipitates from, or alteration products by, paleo-groundwaters or brines, recording the chemical conditions of their formation. Constraining the subsurface brine system that produced them is critical for understanding Mars's aqueous history, geochemical cycles, and past habitability potential.
proposals.hypothesis: The mineralogy, paragenetic sequence, and spatial distribution of sulfate phases observed at the surface can be used to quantitatively model the composition, pH, redox state, and evaporation/cooling pathways of the subsurface brines from which they formed.
proposals.method: We will compile a comprehensive mineralogical dataset from Perseverance instruments (SuperCam, PIXL, SHERLOC) focusing on sulfate occurrences within the Máaz and Séítah formations. Using established thermodynamic models (e.g., FREZCHEM, Geochemist's Workbench) and inverse geochemical modeling codes (PHREEQC), we will perform speciation-solubility calculations. The approach will systematically vary initial brine composition (based on Martian igneous rock weathering) and environmental parameters (T, P_CO2, water:rock ratio) to find the conditions that best reproduce the observed sulfate assemblages and sequences.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce the first quantitative geochemical model of the subsurface brine system at Jezero crater, linking surface mineralogy to subsurface processes. It will provide testable predictions for brine evolution over time and place constraints on the duration and chemical environment of past aqueous activity, directly informing the interpretation of samples cached for future return.
proposals.novelty: While sulfates have been identified, no study has yet used the detailed *assemblages* from Perseverance's high-fidelity instruments in a rigorous inverse modeling framework to deduce the specific properties of the subsurface brine reservoir, moving beyond qualitative descriptions to a quantitative hydrological model.

proposals.title: Constraining Martian Mantle Heat-Producing Element Abundances via Igneous Stratigraphy and Composition at Jezero Crater
proposals.motivation: Models of Martian thermal and magmatic evolution often assume early, efficient crustal differentiation stripped heat-producing elements (HPEs: K, Th, U) from the mantle, leading to a geodynamically inactive planet. However, the igneous stratigraphy and compositional diversity revealed by the Perseverance rover at Jezero Crater, including the Máaz and Séítah formations, suggest a complex magmatic history potentially involving multiple mantle sources or remelting of variably enriched crust. This challenges the paradigm of a simple, globally depleted mantle.
proposals.hypothesis: The compositional and density stratification of igneous units in Jezero crater records magmatic processes involving mantle sources that retained a significant fraction of the planet's heat-producing elements, implying incomplete early crustal extraction and a more geochemically heterogeneous Martian interior than previously modeled.
proposals.method: We will integrate geochemical data from Perseverance (SuperCam, PIXL) on Máaz and Séítah lithologies with pre-2026 experimental petrology and geochemical modeling. A forward geochemical modeling approach will be used, simulating partial melting of mantle sources with varying initial HPE concentrations and residual mineralogy. We will employ trace element mass balance and radiogenic isotope (Sm-Nd, Lu-Hf) evolution models, constrained by the observed igneous stratigraphy and crystallization sequences, to invert for the most probable mantle source HPE abundances and melting conditions that produced the Jezero suite.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first quantitative, geology-grounded estimate of mantle HPE budgets for a specific Martian volcanic province, directly testing global differentiation models. It will establish a new framework for interpreting in-situ igneous stratigraphy as a record of mantle heterogeneity and thermal state, with implications for the timing of volcanism, crustal growth, and potential for sustained mantle convection on Mars.
proposals.novelty: While previous work described Jezero's igneous diversity, this proposal uniquely uses that observed stratification to quantitatively probe mantle composition, moving beyond cataloging crustal rocks to directly interrogate the assumption of a fully depleted Martian mantle.

proposals.title: A Global Test for Martian Crustal Uniformity: High-Resolution Mapping of Potassium and Thorium Abundances from Orbital Gamma-Ray Spectroscopy
proposals.motivation: Pre-2026 orbital gamma-ray data from Mars Odyssey has provided global maps of elemental abundances, but the coarse spatial resolution (~300-600 km) has limited detailed crustal composition analysis. Recent in-situ findings from the Perseverance rover at Jezero crater reveal compositionally stratified igneous units, challenging simple models of a globally uniform basaltic crust. A refined global map of incompatible, heat-producing elements like K and Th is critical to test if the heterogeneity observed at Jezero is a local phenomenon or a fundamental characteristic of the Martian crust, with major implications for crustal formation and thermal evolution models.
proposals.hypothesis: The Martian crust is not globally uniform in composition; instead, it contains large-scale, mappable provinces with distinct K/Th signatures that correlate with major geologic units and ancient terrains, reflecting primary magmatic differentiation and crustal formation processes.
proposals.method: We will apply advanced spatial deconvolution and spectral unmixing algorithms (e.g., the Richardson-Lucy algorithm and pixel-splitting techniques) to the full Mars Odyssey Gamma-Ray Spectrometer (GRS) dataset to improve the effective spatial resolution for K and Th to ~150-200 km. This will involve modeling and subtracting the cosmic-ray background and atmospheric contributions more precisely. The resulting abundance maps will be georegistered and statistically analyzed against pre-2026 geologic and geomorphic maps (e.g., from MOLA and CTX) to identify correlations with crustal provinces, impact basins, and volcanic regions.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce the first high-resolution global maps of potassium and thorium on Mars, directly testing crustal uniformity models. It will establish whether the geochemical diversity seen in Jezero's floor is representative of broader crustal structure, providing a global context for rover findings and new constraints for planetary geochemical and thermal evolution models.
proposals.novelty: While previous GRS studies presented global averages and broad trends, this proposal applies advanced image processing techniques specifically to push the spatial resolution of K and Th mapping beyond the inherent footprint limit of the instrument, enabling a province-scale analysis previously not possible with orbital gamma-ray data.

proposals.title: Quantifying the Role of Early Crustal Enrichment in Suppressing Mantle Melting on Mars: A Geochemical and Geophysical Modeling Study Informed by Jezero Crater Stratigraphy
proposals.motivation: Pre-2026 geochemical and geophysical data from Mars, particularly from the Perseverance rover at Jezero crater, reveal a compositionally stratified early crust with evolved igneous lithologies. This early crustal differentiation likely extracted heat-producing elements (K, U, Th) from the mantle, potentially altering the planet's thermal evolution. Understanding how this crustal enrichment impacted mantle heat flow and melting potential is critical for resolving the paradox of widespread early volcanism versus a rapid decline in magnetic field and tectonic activity.
proposals.hypothesis: The formation of an enriched, stratified early crust, as sampled by the Máaz and Séítah formations at Jezero, sequestered a significant fraction of Mars's radiogenic inventory, leading to a rapid decrease in mantle heat production and adiabatic upwelling, thereby diminishing the possibility of sustained, widespread mantle melting after the Noachian.
proposals.method: We will integrate pre-2026 geochemical data from Martian meteorites, orbital surveys, and Perseverance's analysis of the Máaz and Séítah formations to construct a bulk composition for the early enriched crust. Using this composition in 1-D and 2-D thermal evolution models (e.g., utilizing the codes MarsChaos or similar), we will run coupled mantle convection and melting simulations. The models will specifically test the sensitivity of mantle melting rates to the timing, extent, and geochemistry of early crustal extraction, comparing scenarios with and without this enriched crustal reservoir.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first quantitative link between in-situ observed crustal stratigraphy at Jezero and planetary-scale thermal evolution models. It will deliver a testable mechanism for the rapid decline of Martian mantle melting and magmatic activity, directly connecting surface geology to deep planetary processes. The results will offer a framework for interpreting crustal heterogeneity data from InSight and future missions in the context of mantle dynamics.
proposals.novelty: While early crust formation is known to cool the mantle, this study uniquely quantifies the effect using the specific, measured geochemistry of Mars's best-characterized crustal section at Jezero. It moves beyond generic models by directly tying the geochemical stratification observed by Perseverance to a specific physical mechanism for suppressing subsequent mantle melting.

proposals.title: Constraining the Primordial Magma Ocean and Crustal Stratigraphy of Mars through Integrated Cosmochemical Modeling and In-Situ Igneous Geochemistry
proposals.motivation: Determining the bulk composition of Mars is fundamental for understanding its accretion, core formation, and early magmatic evolution. Recent in-situ data from the Perseverance rover at Jezero crater reveals a compositionally stratified igneous crust (Máaz and Séítah formations), which provides a critical but localized test for global models. A robust bulk composition model is required to connect these local lithologies to planet-scale processes like the crystallization of a primordial magma ocean and the formation of the earliest crust.
proposals.hypothesis: The observed geochemical diversity and stratigraphy of igneous units in Jezero crater can be reproduced by fractional crystallization of melts derived from a bulk Martian mantle, the composition of which is constrained by chondritic accretion models. The Máaz and Séítah formations represent different crystallization products from a common mantle source or from a layered magma ocean cumulate sequence.
proposals.method: We will employ a two-step computational approach. First, we will use mass balance and thermodynamic models (e.g., the *Planetocosm* code) to calculate a range of plausible bulk Mars compositions from carbonaceous and enstatite chondrite building blocks, incorporating core-mantle partitioning from high-pressure experiments. Second, we will use these mantle compositions as starting conditions in the MELTS/alphaMELTS thermodynamic software to model fractional crystallization at relevant pressures (1 bar to 1 GPa). We will specifically test if the major and trace element trends (e.g., Mg#, FeO, incompatible elements) observed by SuperCam and other Perseverance instruments in the Máaz and Séítah formations can be matched by these crystallization pathways.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a quantitative, testable link between cosmochemical theory and the first detailed in-situ igneous stratigraphy on Mars. It will produce a refined set of bulk Mars compositional models directly benchmarked against observational data. The results will offer a petrogenetic framework for the Jezero crater floor units and make specific predictions about the mineralogy and chemistry of deeper crustal layers not yet sampled.
proposals.novelty: While cosmochemical models for Mars exist, this work uniquely integrates them with the new, high-fidelity igneous geochemistry from Perseverance to perform a ground-truth test. The novelty lies in using the observed crustal stratigraphy as a reverse-engineering constraint on accretion and early differentiation models, moving beyond simple geophysical or meteorite-based estimates.

proposals.title: A Lunar-Inspired Gamma-Ray Spectroscopy Calibration Framework for Resolving Compositional Stratigraphy in Jezero Crater Igneous Units
proposals.motivation: Orbital gamma-ray spectroscopy (GRS) is a key tool for mapping the elemental composition of planetary surfaces, but its application to Mars has been hindered by complex calibration challenges related to variable thermal neutron flux and surface roughness. In contrast, lunar GRS data from missions like Lunar Prospector have been extensively validated against returned samples, leading to sophisticated, mature analysis techniques. Applying these refined lunar methods to Martian data, particularly from the Odyssey GRS suite, could significantly improve the accuracy of compositional maps for critical regions like Jezero crater.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that calibration and spectral unmixing algorithms developed for lunar GRS data, when adapted to account for Martian atmospheric and neutron environment differences, will yield more accurate and spatially resolved maps of potassium (K), thorium (Th), and silicon (Si) within Jezero's igneous floor units (e.g., Máaz and Séítah formations), revealing finer-scale compositional stratigraphy linked to magmatic processes.
proposals.method: We will adapt the Lunar Prospector GRS analysis pipeline, specifically its peak-stripping and spatial deconvolution algorithms, to Mars Odyssey GRS data. This involves modifying the neutron correction models using the Mars Odyssey Neutron Spectrometer data to account for the Martian hydrogen-rich regolith and CO2 atmosphere. We will apply these calibrated algorithms to produce high-fidelity K, Th, and Si maps of the Jezero crater region, which will be quantitatively compared to in-situ geochemical data from the Perseverance rover (SuperCam, PIXL) for ground-truth validation. Statistical correlation and principal component analysis will be used to link orbital signatures with the distinct igneous lithologies identified by the rover.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a novel, cross-validated geochemical map of Jezero crater's igneous floor, providing crucial context for Perseverance's cached samples and the crater's volcanic history. It will establish a transferable methodology for enhancing the science return of existing Mars orbital datasets using lunar-derived techniques. The results will directly inform models of magmatic differentiation and crustal stratification in the Jezero region.
proposals.novelty: While lunar and Martian GRS data are often analyzed in isolation, this proposal systematically translates the mature, sample-calibrated lunar analysis framework to Mars, a non-obvious cross-planetary calibration approach. It directly leverages the unique opportunity of having precise ground truth from Perseverance to validate the adapted orbital methods, which was not possible for the Moon during the initial development of these techniques.

proposals.title: Comparative Planetology of Primordial Crusts: Assessing the Prevalence of Early Igneous Stratification on Mars, Earth, Venus, and Mercury
proposals.motivation: Recent findings from the Perseverance rover and InSight lander reveal a compositionally stratified, igneous crust at Jezero crater, Mars, suggesting a complex, potentially global, early magmatic differentiation. Determining whether this is a uniquely Martian phenomenon or a common outcome of terrestrial planet accretion and differentiation is critical for understanding planetary evolution. A systematic comparison with the known early crustal records of Earth (Hadean/Archean terrains), Venus (tessera terrains), and Mercury (intercrater plains and northern smooth plains) can test fundamental models of crustal genesis.
proposals.hypothesis: Early crustal enrichment and large-scale igneous stratification, as observed in Jezero's Máaz and Séítah formations, represent a common but not universal feature of terrestrial planet crustal formation, driven by the efficiency of early magma ocean crystallization and subsequent mantle melting regimes.
proposals.method: We will conduct a comparative geochemical and geophysical analysis using pre-2026 remote sensing and in-situ datasets. For Mars, we will synthesize Perseverance geochemical data (SuperCam, PIXL) and crustal density models from InSight receiver functions. For Earth, we will analyze geochemical compilations of Archean felsic crust and complementary mafic residues. For Venus, we will use Magellan SAR-derived tessera geomorphology and Venus Express VIRTIS emissivity data to infer composition. For Mercury, we will utilize MESSENGER-derived elemental maps (Mg, Si, Al) and geochemical models of its volcanic plains. A principal component analysis of inferred major element chemistry and estimated bulk density will be used to classify crustal types across planets.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce the first unified framework for evaluating the primordial crustal diversity of terrestrial planets, moving beyond single-planet studies. It will directly test whether the processes inferred at Jezero are scalable to a general planetary crust-formation model. The findings will provide critical context for interpreting returned Mars samples and for planning future missions to Venus.
proposals.novelty: While individual planetary crusts are well-studied, a systematic, multi-parameter comparison focusing on early igneous stratification—informed by new, high-fidelity Mars 2020 data—has not been undertaken. This approach explicitly links detailed in-situ findings from a specific Martian location to global-scale processes across the inner solar system.

ideas.title: Quantifying the Duration and Geochemical Legacy of Post-Noachian Hydrologic Oases via Reactive Transport Modeling
ideas.description: This research direction combines the hypothesis of transient post-Noachian habitable oases with advanced groundwater reactive transport modeling to move beyond qualitative assessments. It uses the models to simulate the diagenetic mineral assemblages and isotopic profiles that would form in a sedimentary basin under short-lived (e.g., 10^3-10^5 year) episodes of groundwater upwelling and lake formation. The key synergy is the ability to generate distinct, detectable geochemical fingerprints for 'oasis' durations, which can then be compared to in-situ measurements from rovers (e.g., Curiosity, Perseverance) and orbital data to constrain the timing, frequency, and longevity of these wet episodes, directly testing the inverted climatic view.

ideas.title: Directly measure phosphorus bioavailability in granulite-derived sediments to test their middle crust representativeness
ideas.description: This research direction proposes using direct, high-precision measurements of phosphorus concentration and mineralogical speciation (e.g., apatite, monazite) in modern river sediments draining granulite terrains. The measured phosphorus bioavailability signature is then compared to the expected signature from a theoretical middle crustal protolith versus a lower crustal one. This tests the hypothesis that exposed granulites are representative of the middle crust by providing a quantifiable geochemical link between deep crustal processes and surface nutrient cycling. A mismatch between the measured sediment phosphorus and middle crust models would challenge the representativeness of these terrains and revise our understanding of deep crustal contributions to surface biogeochemistry.

ideas.title: A Multi-Scale Biosignature Detection Framework Integrating Morphological and Geochemical Pattern Recognition for Martian Microbial Mat Fossils
ideas.description: This redesigned system overcomes the ambiguity of morphology-only models by requiring a convergent detection of biosignatures across scales and sensor types. It first uses orbital and rover image data to identify candidate microbial mat structures based on macroscopic texture, then applies stringent pixel-scale spectroscopic analysis to seek diagnostic geochemical patterns linked to mat metabolism and preservation. The framework is trained and validated on an extensive library of terrestrial biotic and abiotic sedimentary structures, forcing the model to discriminate based on combined mineralogical and spatial chemistry features. This integrated approach significantly reduces false positives by demanding that any detection satisfy both morphological and geochemical criteria consistent with a biological origin.

ideas.title: Reconstructing paleo-habitability regimes from the sedimentary record of crustal weathering under extreme climates
ideas.description: This research direction challenges the assumption that sedimentary rocks are a passive, reliable recorder of average upper crustal composition by testing how their geochemical signatures are transformed under the transient acidic or saline conditions proposed as early habitable environments. It investigates whether biosignature preservation and the very processes that create the sedimentary archive are fundamentally biased by these extreme weathering regimes, potentially erasing or overprinting evidence for certain tectonic settings. The synergy allows for a novel reconstruction of paleo-habitability that is grounded in a critical, process-based understanding of the sedimentary proxy itself, moving beyond simple compositional averages to model dynamic crust-environment-life interactions.

A global climate model (GCM) is then run on each ensemble member to simulate the resulting climate. By comparing the GCM outputs (e.g., precipitation patterns, temperature) against the full geological record, the system identifies which global climatic states and boundary conditions can satisfy all available constraints simultaneously, rather than forcing a global model from one local datum.

ideas.title: Adapting Seismic Signal Denoising Techniques for Martian Biosignature Detection
ideas.description: This idea adapts solutions from geophysics, specifically seismic signal denoising and pattern recognition. In seismology, convolutional neural networks (CNNs) are successfully trained on synthetic and field data to distinguish subtle geological signal patterns (like hydrocarbon reservoir indicators) from complex background noise, often using transfer learning from well-characterized sites to new regions. We adapt this by applying similar CNN architectures to process Martian orbital and rover spectral/imagery data. The models would be trained first on high-fidelity synthetic data and terrestrial MISS analogs that have been contaminated with simulated Martian environmental noise (e.g., dust, atmospheric interference, sensor-specific artifacts). The core adaptation is the focus on isolating the weak, spatially correlated spectral and textural signatures of potential biosignatures from the overwhelming abiotic geological noise prevalent in Martian datasets, mirroring the seismic challenge of finding a subtle signal within a noisy background.

ideas.title: Quantify supercontinent-driven phosphorus cycling as a primary control on long-term biospheric evolution
ideas.description: This research integrates the geodynamic modeling of supercontinent cycles with direct, high-resolution geochemical analysis of sedimentary phosphorus. By measuring phosphorus concentrations, speciation, and mineralogy across stratigraphic sequences that span supercontinent assembly and breakup events, the work aims to establish a quantitative record of nutrient flux tied to tectonic modes. This will test the hypothesis that supercontinent assembly sequesters bioavailable phosphorus in continental interiors, while breakup and enhanced weathering liberate it, creating first-order pulses in Earth's biogeochemical cycles. The synergy transforms abstract models of crustal differentiation into a calibrated framework for predicting how tectonic architecture directly governs the long-term habitability and evolutionary trajectory of the biosphere.

ideas.title: Chemistry-agnostic habitability envelopes: Predicting biosignature preservation across extreme pH and salinity gradients
ideas.description: This research moves beyond Earth-like assumptions to develop a first-principles framework for habitability. It uses thermodynamic and kinetic models to define stable envelopes for potential biochemistries across continuous gradients of pH, salinity, and temperature, independent of specific molecular templates. These theoretically derived envelopes are then mapped against the geological and mineralogical records of planetary bodies like Mars and icy moons. The goal is to generate testable, location-specific predictions for where biosignatures from life adapted to permanently or transiently extreme conditions are most likely to be found and preserved.

ideas.title: Adapting Cryo-EM Workflows for In-Situ Nanoscale Martian Mineral-Organic Interaction Analysis
ideas.description: This idea adapts the core workflow from cryo-electron microscopy in structural biology to the study of atomic-scale interactions between clay minerals and organic molecules under Martian conditions. The analogous problem in biology was visualizing fragile, hydrated macromolecular structures without distortion, solved by vitrifying samples and using electron microscopy for 3D reconstruction. The adapted research idea involves designing a simulated Martian environment microreactor that can rapidly freeze (vitrify) mineral-organic interaction events in situ. This preserves the native state of the interactions, allowing for subsequent nanoscale to atomic-scale interrogation using cryo-TEM and spectroscopic techniques to map binding sites, conformational changes, and reaction products under realistic, preserved conditions.

ideas.title: Assessing phosphorus bioavailability as a biosignature of past non-neutral aqueous conditions
ideas.description: This fused research direction proposes to directly measure the concentration, speciation, and mineralogy of sedimentary phosphorus to reconstruct the pH and salinity history of ancient aqueous environments. By analyzing how phosphorus phases (e.g., apatite, vivianite, Fe(III)-phosphates) transform and are preserved under acidic, neutral, or saline conditions, the work tests whether transient non-neutral conditions could have supplied bioavailable phosphorus and preserved microbial biosignatures. This approach synergistically uses phosphorus geochemistry not just as a nutrient metric, but as a direct paleo-environmental proxy to constrain the fluid history that governed both potential habitability and biosignature taphonomy.

ideas.title: High-Resolution Sequence-Stratigraphic Framework for Phosphorus Burial and Remobilization
ideas.description: This research integrates high-resolution sequence stratigraphy with direct, detailed measurements of phosphorus concentration, speciation, and mineralogy within lacustrine sediments. By placing the geochemical data within a precise chronostratigraphic and depositional context, it becomes possible to reconstruct not just *when* phosphorus was buried, but *how* and *where* within the evolving lake basin architecture. This approach allows for the identification of key stratigraphic surfaces (e.g., flooding surfaces, sequence boundaries) and systems tracts (e.g., lowstand vs. highstand deposits) that control phosphorus sequestration efficiency and its subsequent susceptibility to remobilization. The result is a dynamic, four-dimensional model of phosphorus cycling that predicts bioavailability based on depositional history and sedimentary fabric.

ideas.title: Discriminating permanent closure from intermittent outflow in a paleolake using coupled isotopic and geochemical proxies
ideas.description: This research tests the hypothesis that a paleolake, which appears geochemically evolved, achieved its extreme endmembers through sustained evaporative concentration in a predominantly closed basin, rather than through an open hydrological state. It directly addresses the open/closed paradox by employing a suite of proxies sensitive to hydrological intermittency, such as the covariance of oxygen and hydrogen isotopes in sequential mineral phases and the modeling of solute inventories against evaporation pathways. The approach will determine the lake's hydrological stability and identify specific climatic or tectonic drivers that may have triggered rare overflow events, providing a robust, process-based interpretation of its geochemical record.

ideas.title: Disentangling Mantle Production from Preservation Bias in Episodic Crustal Growth
ideas.description: This research integrates high-resolution, data-assimilated geodynamic modeling with a novel preservation simulation to separate true episodic mantle production from artifacts of the incomplete rock record. The core method involves generating synthetic crustal production histories from mantle convection models and passing them through a physics-based filter that simulates the destruction and obscuration of crust over time. By iteratively comparing these filtered model outputs to the global geochronological and isotopic database, the system quantitatively deconvolves the preserved signal to reveal the underlying rates and triggers of crust formation. This approach provides a robust, testable framework to distinguish between real mantle events and sampling bias, fundamentally improving estimates of crustal growth timing and triggers.

ideas.title: A Diagenesis-Aware, Multi-Proxy Framework for Deconvolving Primary Redox Signals from Post-Depositional Overprint in Stratigraphic Sequences
ideas.description: This research integrates high-resolution micro-analytical mapping of redox-sensitive elements and isotopes with independent petrographic and geochemical constraints on diagenetic history to discriminate primary depositional signals from secondary overprints. The approach systematically pairs analyses targeting the original redox state (e.g., iron speciation, trace metal enrichments within preserved primary fabrics) with direct indicators of diagenetic processes (e.g., paragenetic sequences, carbonate clumped isotopes, fluid inclusion microthermometry). This dual-proxy framework allows for the robust reconstruction of evolving paleoredox conditions by identifying and, where possible, quantitatively correcting for the effects of post-depositional alteration within a vertical stratigraphic column.

ideas.title: Challenging the Primordial Monoculture: Alternative Metabolic Foundations for Early Biospheres
ideas.description: This idea challenges the core assumption in origins-of-life research that chemolithoautotrophy—metabolism deriving energy from inorganic chemicals and carbon from CO2—was the sole viable foundation for a primordial biosphere. By drawing an analogy from astrobiology, where the assumption that liquid water was the only possible solvent for life was overturned by systematically analyzing alternative solvents, we propose a similar methodological shift. The adapted research idea involves moving beyond the specific case for chemolithoautotrophy to model the 'metabolic habitability' of early planetary environments. This entails systematically evaluating the thermodynamic and kinetic feasibility of alternative foundational metabolisms—such as primitive phototrophy using ambient light and simple pigments, or early heterotrophy on abiotically synthesized organic compounds—under realistic constraints of early Earth or other rocky planets. The outcome would be a framework that defines the environmental conditions (e.g., energy flux, substrate availability, temperature, pH) under which different metabolic foundations become viable, thereby challenging the notion of a single, necessary starting point and expanding the possible narratives for biosphere emergence.

ideas.title: Apply Blind Source Separation from Signal Processing to XRD and Geochemistry
ideas.description: This idea adapts a solution from signal processing and audio engineering to the problem of deconvolving mixed signals in geological data. In audio, blind source separation (BSS) techniques like Independent Component Analysis (ICA) are used to isolate individual sound sources from a single mixed recording. We propose applying these same multivariate statistical methods to X-ray diffraction (XRD) patterns and bulk geochemistry, treating the measured data as a linear mixture of signals from pure mineral phases and distinct environmental processes (e.g., weathering, diagenesis). By statistically identifying and separating these latent source signals without requiring a complete reference library, this approach could more effectively deconvolve complex mineralogy and isolate the geochemical fingerprints of specific environmental events in sedimentary or soil systems.

ideas.title: Applying Metabolic Network Analysis to Supercontinent-Driven Crustal Differentiation
ideas.description: This research idea adapts constraint-based network analysis from systems biology to geology. The analogous problem—predicting how a cell's internal composition changes in response to environmental shifts—is solved by modeling metabolism as a reaction network and calculating flux distributions. Here, we model the supercontinent cycle as a planetary-scale tectonic network. By defining crust and mantle reservoirs and the mass-transfer processes (subduction, magmatism, collision, etc.) that connect them, we can apply mass-balance and thermodynamic constraints. This allows us to simulate how the pulsating tectonic activity of supercontinent assembly and breakup acts as a pump, systematically differentiating the crust's chemical and isotopic composition over billion-year timescales, providing a quantitative framework to link tectonic cycles to the geochemical record.

ideas.title: Applying Epidemiological Models to Episodic Crustal Growth
ideas.description: This research idea adapts solution methods from epidemiology to quantify episodic crustal growth. In epidemiology, the timing, rates, and triggers of disease outbreaks are determined using compartmental models fitted to time-series data. Analogously, we propose to treat episodes of continental crust formation as 'outbreaks' in the geological record. By developing a similar statistical modeling framework—where crustal volume is the cumulative 'infected' population, mantle reservoirs are 'susceptible' or 'source' populations, and specific geodynamic events are 'transmission vectors'—we can fit models to the global distribution of crust formation ages (e.g., from detrital zircon U-Pb data). This approach will formally quantify the rates of episodic growth pulses, statistically identify the most probable mantle processes (e.g., plume episodes, subduction avalanches) responsible for each pulse, and evaluate how the 'susceptibility' of the mantle system to crust-producing events has changed throughout Earth history.

ideas.title: Hydrological stability and extreme geochemistry as drivers of sustained biogenic element bioavailability in a lacustrine system
ideas.description: This research direction investigates the hypothesis that a hydrologically open and stable lake system, despite exhibiting extreme evaporative geochemical endmembers (e.g., high salinity, pH extremes), could maintain a dynamic equilibrium that promotes the continuous bioavailability of biogenic elements like sulfur, nitrogen, and phosphorus. It moves beyond testing simple presence/sequestration to model how stable hydrology (through inflow, outflow, and mixing) interacts with extreme geochemistry to cycle elements between mineral-bound and bioaccessible reservoirs. The synergy lies in evaluating whether hydrological stability provides a persistent mechanism to counteract the sequestering effects of extreme mineral precipitation, thereby creating a potentially habitable niche with sustained nutrient availability over long periods.

ideas.title: Adapting the 'Stellar Feedback Drives Galactic Structure' Analogy to Crustal Dynamics
ideas.description: This idea inverts the assumed causality in supercontinent cycles by drawing an analogy from galactic astrophysics. In that field, the paradigm shifted from galactic structure dictating star formation to recognizing that localized, episodic stellar feedback drives large-scale structural change. Adapting this solution, we propose investigating whether episodic crustal growth pulses (analogous to stellar feedback) are the primary driver of supercontinent cycles, not the consequence. The adapted research would test if the timing, geochemistry, and thermal impact of major crustal addition events—such as from large igneous provinces or accretionary orogens—fundamentally reorganize mantle convection patterns and plate boundary forces. This would provide the bottom-up mechanistic trigger that forces continents to coalesce and later rift apart, inverting the traditional top-down model where the supercontinent cycle itself schedules crustal growth.

ideas.title: Quantitative Speciation and Bioaccessibility Profiling of Biogenic Elements
ideas.description: This research systematically quantifies the specific chemical forms (species) of biogenic elements like S, N, P, and Fe in geological samples to determine true bioaccessibility. It employs a tiered analytical protocol combining sequential extractions to operationally define bioavailable pools with direct spectroscopic speciation to identify mineral hosts and redox states. The output is a quantitative profile for each element, detailing the percentage present in immediately soluble, microbially reducible, or permanently sequestered phases. This moves beyond bulk concentration to a functional assessment of elemental bioavailability for putative early life.

ideas.title: Modeling crustal differentiation as a reactive heat exchanger system
ideas.description: This research adapts solution methods from chemical engineering reactor design to quantify the thermal effects of basaltic underplating on crustal differentiation. The analogous problem—modeling heat and mass transfer in a reactive, porous medium like a catalytic packed-bed reactor—is solved by coupling energy, mass, and momentum balance equations. We adapt this by conceptualizing the crust as a reactive heat exchanger: underplated basaltic magma is the hot fluid, crustal rocks are the reactive solid matrix with defined solidi and latent heats, and granite generation is the product of endothermic melting reactions. The adapted research idea involves developing a numerical model that simultaneously solves for the advection and conduction of heat from the basalt, the temperature-dependent partial melting of crustal lithologies consuming energy, and the segregation and transport of the generated granitic melts, providing a quantitative, process-based framework for this fundamental petrogenetic mechanism.

ideas.title: A Data Assimilation Framework for Lower Crustal Composition
ideas.description: This research adapts the data assimilation framework from climate science and numerical weather prediction to solve the analogous problem of integrating sparse, heterogeneous data to constrain a hidden system state. In climate science, models assimilate diverse data streams to produce optimal atmospheric analyses. Here, we propose developing a formal data assimilation (or inverse) framework for the lower crust. A geochemical (thermodynamic/phase equilibria) and geophysical (thermoelastic) model provides the prior prediction of lower crustal state. Diverse observational datasets—including xenolith major/trace element chemistry, seismic velocity (Vp, Vs) and density profiles from receiver functions and refraction studies, and experimental data on mineral stability and physical properties—are ingested as constraints. An inverse method (e.g., Bayesian Monte Carlo, variational assimilation) is then used to find the global average bulk composition (and its uncertainty) that provides the optimal, physically consistent fit to all data types, moving beyond simple averaging or qualitative comparison.

ideas.title: Sedimentary Source Inversion via Geochemical Fingerprinting and Bayesian Unmixing
ideas.description: This research adapts the inverse spectral unmixing approach used in astrophysics to infer the geology of a catchment from its lake sediments. In astrophysics, the mixed-light spectrum of a celestial body is decomposed into contributions from known mineral or stellar components using linear unmixing and Bayesian methods to solve the underdetermined inverse problem. Here, the bulk geochemical or mineralogical composition of lake sediments is treated as the mixed signal. By constructing a library of geochemical 'fingerprints' for potential source bedrock units in the region, a Bayesian unmixing model is applied to calculate the posterior probability distribution for the proportional contribution of each rock type. The solution provides a probabilistic reconstruction of catchment lithology and dominant weathering processes, complete with uncertainty quantification, turning sediment analysis into a quantitative tool for remote geological inference.

ideas.title: Applying Seismic Stratigraphy's Wheeler Diagram Concept to Lacustrine Sequence Stratigraphy
ideas.description: This idea adapts a core methodological tool from seismic stratigraphy—the Wheeler diagram (chronostratigraphic chart)—to the context of modeling lake-level fluctuations and sediment depositional cycles. In marine geophysics, Wheeler diagrams transform spatial seismic data into a time-stratigraphic domain to visualize deposition, hiatus, and erosion. The adapted research idea applies this conceptual framework to lacustrine systems. By constructing high-resolution Wheeler diagrams for lake basins using precise chronologies (e.g., from varves, radiocarbon, or tuned climate cycles), researchers can explicitly map the timing and duration of depositional cycles, flooding events, and stratigraphic gaps. This will provide a powerful visual and analytical model to disentangle the complex interplay of climate-driven lake-level change, sediment supply, and basin subsidence in forming the sedimentary archive.

ideas.title: Physics-Constrained Adversarial Chemometrics for XRD-Geochemistry Data Fusion
ideas.description: This approach fuses multivariate analysis of XRD patterns and bulk geochemistry within a physics-informed adversarial machine learning framework. The system is designed to deconvolve mixed signals by leveraging statistical patterns while strictly adhering to thermodynamic stability, mineral stoichiometry, and known crystal-chemical rules. An adversarial critic network, trained on extensive mineralogical and phase equilibrium databases, continuously evaluates proposed deconvolutions to filter out geochemically impossible solutions. This results in robust, physically realistic interpretations of complex mineral assemblages and their environmental forming conditions from heterogeneous natural samples.

ideas.title: Constraining reactive transport models with direct P speciation to predict diagenetic phosphorus remobilization
ideas.description: This research integrates direct, high-resolution measurements of phosphorus concentration, speciation, and mineralogy from sediment cores with coupled hydrogeochemical reactive transport models. The direct measurements provide critical initial and boundary conditions, as well as validation data, for the diagenetic models. This synergy allows the models to move beyond generic simulations to predict the rates and pathways of phosphorus transformation and remobilization in response to specific perturbations, such as water level changes or redox shifts. The outcome is a powerful tool for quantitatively forecasting future phosphorus bioavailability and eutrophication risk in lake systems.

ideas.title: Adapt cellular metabolic network models for sediment diagenesis
ideas.description: This idea proposes adapting the constraint-based modeling approach from systems biology, specifically genome-scale metabolic modeling (GEM) and flux balance analysis (FBA), to the problem of simulating diagenetic alteration in lake sediments. In the analogous field, complex networks of biochemical reactions are simulated by defining a stoichiometric matrix of all possible reactions and solving for flux distributions that satisfy mass balance and thermodynamic constraints under given boundary conditions (e.g., nutrient availability). This solution is adapted by conceptualizing the sediment column as a geochemical 'reaction network' where mineral dissolution, precipitation, and redox reactions are nodes connected by solute transport. The coupled hydrogeochemical reactive transport model would be reformulated to solve for a feasible set of diagenetic reaction rates that satisfy mass conservation and energy gradients, rather than relying solely on predefined kinetic rate laws. This could reveal emergent, stable diagenetic states and mineral assemblages constrained by the system's physicochemical boundaries.

ideas.title: Constrained inverse reactive transport modeling of lacustrine diagenesis using multi-proxy data assimilation
ideas.description: This research develops a coupled inverse modeling framework that integrates a hydrogeochemical reactive transport simulator with a parameter estimation algorithm. The system is designed to overcome equifinality by using high-resolution field data—including mineralogy from XRD, porewater chemistry from squeeze extracts, and isotopic signatures—as quantitative calibration targets. The approach iteratively tests thousands of possible flow and reaction scenarios, identifying parameter sets that produce simulations consistent with the observed diagenetic stratigraphy. This results in a robust, probabilistically constrained reconstruction of the paleo-groundwater flow regime and its role in sediment alteration, moving from speculative simulation to data-validated interpretation.

ideas.title: Challenging the Closed-Basin Assumption with a Metabolic Network Analogy
ideas.description: The original idea challenges the assumption that a paleolake with extreme geochemical endmembers must have been a hydrologically closed, evaporative basin. By drawing an analogy from systems biology, specifically metabolic network analysis, we adapt a solution where systems assumed to be closed are re-evaluated as open but regulated networks. In biology, constraint-based modeling identifies minimal exchange fluxes needed to maintain stable, extreme internal states. Adapted to hydrology, this involves constructing a coupled water-and-solute flux model for the lake basin. The model tests the stability of the extreme geochemical endmembers under various 'open' network configurations with constrained, low-magnitude inflows/outflows (e.g., episodic groundwater exchange). The goal is to identify the simplest open-system model that is consistent with the geochemical data, providing a viable alternative hypothesis to the closed-basin paradigm.

ideas.title: Anchored Multi-Archive Reconstruction of Lacustrine System Duration and Climatic Pacing
ideas.description: This research integrates the target fluvio-lacustrine sedimentary record with precisely dated independent climate archives (e.g., speleothems, marine cores) and multiple geochronometers within the basin to construct a robust, anchored timeline. The approach cross-correlates physical, geochemical, and biological proxies across these records to identify and quantify true climatic cycles versus local sedimentary hiatuses or autogenic processes. This multi-archive framework quantitatively constrains the total duration, episodicity, and pacing of lake environment phases with significantly reduced uncertainty, providing a regionally contextualized paleoclimatic history.

ideas.title: Process-Constrained Forward Stratigraphic Modeling of Lacustrine Sequences
ideas.description: This research integrates high-resolution sequence stratigraphic analysis with a dedicated lacustrine forward stratigraphic model (FSM) to deconvolve the controls on lake-level cycles. The FSM is explicitly driven by quantitative inputs for climatic precipitation/evaporation, catchment sediment yield, and tectonic subsidence, generating synthetic stratigraphy for comparison with observed sequences. This model-data iterative loop tests sequence stratigraphic interpretations, quantifies the relative impact of different drivers, and produces a robust, process-understood depositional model that predicts facies architecture beyond the limits of correlation alone.

ideas.title: Microfluidic Chemostat Array for Simulating Lake Microbial Niches
ideas.description: This research adapts the principle of laboratory-scale chemostats from microbial ecology and bioreactor engineering to the microfluidic simulation of lake chemistry. The analogous solution uses continuous-flow bioreactors to maintain steady-state chemical gradients for studying microbial dynamics. The adapted idea is to create a multiplexed microfluidic 'lake-chemostat' array. Each microfluidic chamber functions as an independent microchemostat, with precisely controlled inflows and outflows of synthetic lake water to establish and maintain stable chemical gradients that mimic specific microenvironments within a lake, such as oxic/anoxic boundaries or sediment-water interfaces. This platform enables the high-throughput, real-time investigation of chemical reaction kinetics, nutrient cycling, and the response of microbial consortia to persistent, spatially-defined chemical conditions at a microscale.
